With 7/10 pain, starting with pain modulation (Grade I-II) is more appropriate before progressing to stretching mobilizations. However, capsular restriction requires stretching (Grade III-IV eventually). Given the significant restriction, Grade III mobilizations are appropriate if tolerated. The question asks what's MOST appropriate now - with 7/10 pain, Grade I-II would be better initially. But adhesive capsulitis requires capsular stretching. I'll say B is correct for the restriction, though pain management is important.

questionText: An infant born at 28 weeks gestational age (now 6 months corrected age) demonstrates asymmetric tonic neck reflex, absent head righting, and lower extremity scissoring. What condition is MOST likely?

options.B: Developmental coordination disorder
options.C: Cerebral palsy, spastic diplegia
options.D: Muscular dystrophy

explanation: Option C is correct. Premature infants have increased risk of cerebral palsy. Persistent ATNR at 6 months corrected age, absent head righting (should be present by 4-6 months), and scissoring (hip adductor spasticity) indicate spastic CP, likely diplegic pattern (common in prematurity). Option A is incorrect; these are abnormal findings. Option B is diagnosed in older children with coordination difficulties, not infants with primitive reflex retention. Option D shows progressive weakness without spasticity or reflex abnormalities.

questionText: A 66-year-old patient is recovering from COVID-19 pneumonia with persistent dyspnea. Pulmonary function tests show reduced DLCO (diffusion capacity) but normal spirometry. What does this finding suggest?

options.B: Impaired gas exchange/interstitial involvement
options.C: Restrictive chest wall disorder
options.D: Upper airway obstruction

explanation: Option B is correct. Reduced DLCO (diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide) with normal spirometry indicates impaired gas exchange at the alveolar-capillary membrane, suggesting interstitial lung involvement, pulmonary vascular disease, or emphysema. This is common after COVID-19 pneumonia due to alveolar damage and fibrosis. Option A would show reduced FEV1/FVC ratio. Option C would show reduced lung volumes on spirometry. Option D would show flow-volume loop abnormalities.

questionText: A 45-year-old patient with a Stage 4 pressure injury over the ischium shows exposed bone and undermining. After 3 weeks of treatment, the wound bed appears clean with granulation tissue, but has not reduced in size. What is the MOST appropriate intervention?

options.A: Continue current treatment as granulation indicates healing
options.B: Apply silver sulfadiazine for infection control
options.C: Increase the frequency of dressing changes
options.D: Refer to physician/wound specialist for surgical evaluation (debridement, flap closure)

explanation: Option D is correct. Stage 4 pressure injuries with exposed bone rarely heal by secondary intention alone. Surgical intervention (debridement of necrotic bone, muscle/fasciocutaneous flap coverage) is typically required for closure. Clean granulation tissue indicates readiness for surgical closure. Conservative treatment alone for Stage 4 with bone involvement has poor outcomes. Options A-C represent continued conservative care that is insufficient for this severity of wound.

questionText: A 33-year-old runner presents with sharp anterior hip pain that began during a race. The patient has limited hip extension (0 degrees), positive FABER test, and pain with resisted hip flexion. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Hip flexor strain (iliopsoas)
options.C: Sports hernia (athletic pubalgia)
options.D: Acetabular labral tear

explanation: Option B is correct. Acute onset during running, limited hip extension (tight hip flexors), positive FABER (flexion, abduction, external rotation test), and pain with resisted hip flexion all indicate hip flexor/iliopsoas strain. Option A would show groin pain with weight-bearing, hop test pain, and requires imaging. Option C causes lower abdominal/groin pain worse with cutting and kicking movements. Option D typically has mechanical symptoms (clicking, catching) and positive impingement tests.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient presents with gradual onset of bilateral foot drop, steppage gait, and distal leg muscle atrophy. Sensation is intact, and upper extremities are unaffected. EMG shows denervation. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Peripheral vascular disease
options.C: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
options.D: Diabetic neuropathy

explanation: Option C is correct. Bilateral progressive foot drop with distal leg atrophy, steppage gait, preserved sensation (in some types), and EMG denervation pattern suggests hereditary motor sensory neuropathy like Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease. Bilateral presentation rules out unilateral radiculopathy. Option A (L5 radiculopathy) would be unilateral. Option B doesn't cause foot drop or denervation. Option D usually has sensory symptoms and doesn't typically cause isolated bilateral foot drop.

questionText: A 56-year-old patient with newly diagnosed hypertension (BP 156/94 mmHg) asks about exercise recommendations. The patient is sedentary and otherwise healthy. What is the MOST appropriate exercise prescription?

options.A: Avoid all exercise until blood pressure is controlled with medication
options.B: Moderate intensity aerobic exercise (40-60% VO2max) most days of the week for 30-60 minutes
options.C: High-intensity resistance training only to build muscle mass
options.D: Isometric exercises to strengthen core muscles

explanation: Option B is correct. Moderate-intensity aerobic exercise is first-line therapy for Stage 1 hypertension (SBP 130-139 or DBP 80-89) and can reduce BP by 5-8 mmHg. This patient's BP (156/94) is Stage 2, but exercise remains beneficial alongside medication. Guidelines recommend 150 minutes/week of moderate aerobic activity. Option A is incorrect; exercise is therapeutic, not contraindicated. Option C alone is insufficient; aerobic exercise has greater BP-lowering effect. Option D (isometric) can acutely raise BP and isn't primary recommendation.

questionText: A 38-year-old pregnant patient at 30 weeks gestation reports lower back pain and pelvic pain. Examination reveals positive posterior pelvic pain provocation test and pain with single-leg stance. What exercise is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Pelvic stabilization exercises targeting transversus abdominis and pelvic floor muscles
options.B: Aggressive lumbar flexion exercises
options.C: High-impact aerobic exercises for cardiovascular fitness
options.D: Supine abdominal strengthening exercises

explanation: Option A is correct. Positive PPPP test indicates sacroiliac joint dysfunction, common in pregnancy due to hormonal ligamentous laxity. Pelvic stabilization exercises (transversus abdominis, pelvic floor, hip stabilizers) provide dynamic support and reduce pain. Option B may increase SIJ stress. Option C creates excessive impact forces on already-stressed joints. Option D is contraindicated after 20 weeks gestation due to supine hypotensive syndrome from aortocaval compression.

questionText: A 19-year-old basketball player sustained an ankle inversion injury 48 hours ago. Examination reveals significant swelling, ecchymosis, 8mm anterior drawer, positive talar tilt (15 degrees), and inability to bear weight. What is the MOST appropriate management?

options.A: Immediate ankle mobilizations to prevent stiffness
options.B: Full weight-bearing with functional ankle taping only
options.C: RICE protocol, protected weight-bearing with boot/brace, and orthopedic referral for possible surgical consultation
options.D: Aggressive strengthening exercises beginning immediately

explanation: Option C is correct. Positive anterior drawer AND talar tilt with significant measurements (>8mm, >10-15 degrees difference) suggests Grade III ankle sprain with complete ligament tears (ATFL and CFL). This severity, especially in young athletes, may warrant surgical consideration. Initial management includes RICE, protected weight-bearing, and orthopedic evaluation. Options A, B, and D are too aggressive for acute severe injury and could worsen instability.

questionText: A 65-year-old patient with diabetic peripheral neuropathy scores 15/28 on the Berg Balance Scale. What is the MOST appropriate intervention focus?

options.A: High-level balance activities like tandem walking and single-leg stance
options.B: Bed mobility exercises only due to high fall risk
options.C: Vestibular rehabilitation exercises
options.D: Progressive balance training with appropriate safety measures and environmental modifications; fall prevention education

explanation: Option D is correct. Berg Balance Scale score <20/28 indicates high fall risk (15/28 is moderate-high risk). Intervention should include progressive balance training at appropriate level with safety measures (gait belt, parallel bars), environmental modifications (remove hazards, improve lighting), assistive device assessment, and comprehensive fall prevention education. Option A is too advanced and unsafe. Option B is too restrictive and increases deconditioning. Option C addresses vestibular causes, not the peripheral neuropathy causing sensory loss.

questionText: A 70-year-old patient with emphysema demonstrates barrel chest, use of accessory muscles at rest, and pursed-lip breathing. Pulmonary function tests show increased total lung capacity and residual volume. What is the PRIMARY mechanism of dyspnea?

options.B: Air trapping and hyperinflation reducing diaphragm efficiency
options.C: Pulmonary fibrosis restricting expansion
options.D: Pleural effusion limiting lung expansion

explanation: Option B is correct. Emphysema causes loss of elastic recoil, leading to premature small airway closure during exhalation, air trapping, and hyperinflation. This flattens the diaphragm, placing it at mechanical disadvantage and requiring accessory muscle recruitment. Increased TLC and RV confirm hyperinflation. Option A is opposite; emphysema increases compliance. Option C describes restriction, not obstruction. Option D is not indicated by this presentation.

questionText: A 72-year-old patient with a chronic arterial ulcer on the lateral malleolus has an ABI of 0.62. Pedal pulses are diminished. What intervention is CONTRAINDICATED?

options.B: Protective padding and pressure relief
options.C: Gentle range of motion exercises
options.D: Referral to vascular surgeon for revascularization evaluation

explanation: Option A is correct as the CONTRAINDICATED intervention. ABI <0.8 indicates peripheral arterial disease; ABI <0.5 is severe. Compression therapy is absolutely contraindicated with ABI <0.8 as it can further compromise arterial blood flow, causing tissue ischemia and necrosis. Options B, C, and D are all appropriate: protective measures prevent trauma, gentle ROM maintains joint mobility without increasing metabolic demand, and vascular evaluation is essential for revascularization to improve healing potential.

questionText: A 48-year-old patient presents with gradual onset of hand numbness, thenar atrophy, and difficulty opening jars. Tinel's sign is positive at the wrist, Phalen's test positive at 45 seconds, and two-point discrimination is 8mm on thumb. What does the two-point discrimination finding indicate?

options.B: Mild sensory impairment
options.C: Moderate to severe sensory impairment
options.D: Complete sensory loss

explanation: Option C is correct. Normal two-point discrimination in fingertips is 2-5mm. 6-10mm indicates moderate sensory impairment, and >10mm indicates severe impairment or loss of protective sensation. At 8mm, this patient has moderate sensory deficit. Combined with thenar atrophy, this suggests moderate-to-severe carpal tunnel syndrome requiring urgent treatment to prevent permanent nerve damage. Options A and B underestimate severity. Option D would be inability to detect any two points (>15mm).

questionText: A 5-year-old child with Down syndrome demonstrates hypotonia, joint laxity, and delayed motor milestones. The child walks independently but has difficulty with stairs. What is the MOST important consideration for physical therapy?

options.A: Focus solely on strength training to overcome hypotonia
options.B: Screen for atlantoaxial instability before implementing exercise program; focus on motor skill development and strengthening
options.C: Aggressive stretching to improve joint range of motion
options.D: Limit all physical activity due to ligamentous laxity

explanation: Option B is correct. Children with Down syndrome require atlantoaxial instability screening (clinical exam, possibly radiographs) before beginning therapy due to increased ligamentous laxity. Once cleared, therapy focuses on motor skill development and appropriate strengthening. Option A ignores critical safety screening and oversimplifies intervention. Option C is counterproductive with existing hypermobility. Option D is too restrictive; appropriate physical activity is essential for development.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient presents to outpatient therapy with complaints of dyspnea and chest tightness that occurred during yesterday's exercise session. The patient took sublingual nitroglycerin with relief. What is the MOST appropriate action TODAY?

options.A: Do not treat; refer immediately to physician for cardiac evaluation before resuming therapy
options.B: Proceed with therapy but at lower intensity and monitor closely
options.C: Perform graded exercise test to determine functional capacity
options.D: Continue with planned treatment as symptoms resolved with nitroglycerin

explanation: Option A is correct. New onset exercise-induced chest tightness requiring nitroglycerin suggests possible unstable angina or other cardiac pathology requiring immediate medical evaluation. Physical therapy must not proceed until medical clearance is obtained. This could represent acute coronary syndrome. Options B, C, and D all inappropriately continue therapy with a patient showing signs of cardiac ischemia. Patient safety requires immediate physician referral and medical management before any further exercise.

questionText: A 55-year-old patient with chronic lymphedema of the right upper extremity following breast cancer treatment asks about exercise. What recommendation is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Avoid all upper extremity exercise to prevent lymphedema exacerbation
options.B: High-intensity resistance training to maximize strength gains
options.C: Gradual progressive resistance training with compression garment use and careful monitoring for swelling
options.D: Aquatic therapy only due to hydrostatic pressure benefits

explanation: Option C is correct. Current evidence shows that gradual progressive resistance training is safe and beneficial for lymphedema patients when performed with compression garments and careful monitoring. Exercise improves function and quality of life without worsening lymphedema when properly progressed. Option A is outdated; complete activity avoidance leads to deconditioning. Option B is too aggressive initially; gradual progression is key. Option D is too restrictive; while aquatic therapy is beneficial, land-based exercise is also appropriate.

questionText: A 30-year-old patient presents with clicking and catching in the knee during squatting, positive McMurray's test, and joint line tenderness. MRI confirms medial meniscus tear. Conservative treatment for 6 weeks has failed. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Continue conservative treatment for 6 more months before considering surgery
options.B: Referral to orthopedic surgeon for arthroscopic evaluation/repair
options.C: Aggressive strengthening to compensate for mechanical symptoms
options.D: Corticosteroid injection into the knee joint

explanation: Option B is correct. Meniscal tears with mechanical symptoms (locking, catching, giving way) that fail conservative treatment typically require arthroscopic intervention. Young, active patients with mechanical symptoms are good surgical candidates. Continued mechanical symptoms indicate displaced meniscus fragment requiring debridement or repair. Option A delays necessary intervention. Option C cannot address mechanical blocking. Option D provides temporary pain relief but doesn't resolve mechanical problem.

questionText: A 76-year-old patient with Alzheimer's disease (moderate stage) is receiving physical therapy. The patient becomes agitated and combative during transfers. What is the MOST appropriate modification?

options.A: Physically restrain the patient to complete the transfer safely
options.B: Discontinue all physical therapy due to behavioral issues
options.C: Medicate the patient before all therapy sessions
options.D: Simplify instructions, reduce environmental stimuli, allow breaks, and approach from front with calm manner

explanation: Option D is correct. Behavioral management in dementia includes: simple one-step instructions, reduced environmental stimulation, consistent routine, calm approach, visual cueing, allowing time to process, and building on preserved abilities. Approaching from front prevents startling. Option A is inappropriate and potentially harmful; restraints increase agitation. Option B eliminates beneficial therapy. Option C should be last resort and requires physician order; behavioral interventions should be tried first.

questionText: A 45-year-old patient with asthma uses a rescue inhaler before exercise sessions. During exercise today, the patient experiences increased wheezing, chest tightness, and SpO2 drops to 89%. What is the MOST appropriate immediate action?

options.B: Stop exercise, administer bronchodilator, position for optimal breathing, monitor closely, and activate EMS if no improvement
options.C: Place patient supine to reduce work of breathing
options.D: Provide supplemental oxygen and continue exercise

explanation: Option B is correct. Acute asthma exacerbation during exercise requires immediate intervention: stop activity, administer rescue bronchodilator, position for breathing (sitting upright/leaning forward), monitor response, and activate emergency services if severe or not responsive to bronchodilator. SpO2 89% with symptoms indicates significant bronchoconstriction. Option A continues stress on compromised respiratory system. Option C worsens breathing mechanics. Option D doesn't address bronchospasm, the primary problem.

questionText: A 68-year-old patient has a venous ulcer that has been treated with compression therapy for 8 weeks with good healing progress. The wound bed is now 90% epithelialized. What is the MOST appropriate modification to the treatment plan?

options.A: Discontinue compression therapy as wound is nearly healed
options.B: Increase compression to 50 mmHg to accelerate final healing
options.C: Continue compression therapy and begin transition planning for long-term compression stockings for prevention
options.D: Switch to negative pressure wound therapy for final closure

explanation: Option C is correct. Compression therapy must continue throughout healing and lifelong for venous insufficiency management. As wound nears closure, planning for transition to maintenance compression stockings (20-30 mmHg) is essential to prevent recurrence. Venous hypertension is chronic condition requiring ongoing management. Option A leads to recurrence; 60-70% of venous ulcers recur without compression. Option B exceeds therapeutic compression levels. Option D is unnecessary for well-healing wound.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with lumbar spinal stenosis reports bilateral leg pain and numbness that worsens with walking and improves with sitting/forward flexion. What exercise position would be MOST beneficial?

options.A: Flexion-based exercises including pelvic tilts and knee-to-chest stretches
options.B: Extension-based exercises including prone press-ups
options.C: Neutral spine exercises only, avoiding all flexion and extension
options.D: Rotation exercises to improve spinal mobility

explanation: Option A is correct. Lumbar spinal stenosis symptoms (neurogenic claudication) worsen with extension and improve with flexion due to changes in spinal canal diameter. Flexion opens the canal, while extension narrows it. Flexion-based exercises are therapeutic for stenosis. Option B (extension) would exacerbate symptoms by further narrowing the canal. Option C is overly restrictive; flexion is therapeutic. Option D doesn't address the primary pathomechanics of stenosis.

questionText: A 25-year-old patient sustained complete C7 spinal cord injury 8 weeks ago. What is the HIGHEST level of independence expected for transfers?

options.A: Dependent for all transfers requiring mechanical lift
options.B: Minimal assist for transfers with sliding board
options.C: Independent level transfers with sliding board
options.D: Independent with all transfers including floor-to-wheelchair

explanation: Option C is correct. C7 SCI preserves triceps, allowing controlled elbow extension for depression transfers. Patients can achieve independent level transfers (wheelchair-to-bed, wheelchair-to-car) using sliding board. Option A underestimates C7 function; this would be appropriate for C5-C6. Option B is also too conservative for C7. Option D overestimates ability; floor-to-wheelchair transfers typically require C8-T1 level for full hand function and ability to lift body weight from floor.

questionText: A 52-year-old patient with sarcoidosis affecting the lungs demonstrates restrictive pattern on PFTs. The patient reports dyspnea with ADLs. What energy conservation technique should be PRIORITIZED in education?

options.A: Rapid completion of tasks to minimize total exertion time
options.B: Pacing activities with scheduled rest periods and exhaling during exertion
options.C: Breath-holding during difficult tasks to stabilize the trunk
options.D: Performing all ADLs in supine position to reduce cardiac demand

explanation: Option B is correct. Energy conservation for pulmonary patients includes pacing activities, scheduling rest periods, coordinating breathing with activity (exhaling during exertion), prioritizing tasks, and using assistive devices. This maximizes function while managing dyspnea. Option A increases metabolic demand and dyspnea. Option C (Valsalva) is contraindicated; increases intrathoracic pressure and cardiac stress. Option D is impractical and doesn't address breathing efficiency.

questionText: A 44-year-old patient with rheumatoid arthritis reports severe morning stiffness lasting 2 hours and fatigue. Lab results show elevated ESR and CRP. What is the MOST appropriate exercise timing and approach?

options.A: Avoid all exercise during disease flares
options.B: High-intensity exercise in the morning to work through stiffness
options.C: Gentle ROM and low-impact aerobic exercise later in day after morning stiffness resolves; avoid exercise during acute flares
options.D: Aggressive strengthening only when inflammation is present

explanation: Option C is correct. During active RA (elevated inflammatory markers, prolonged morning stiffness), exercise should be gentle and performed when stiffness has decreased (later in day or after heat/medication). Gentle ROM maintains mobility, and low-impact aerobic exercise is beneficial. Avoid aggressive exercise during acute flares but maintain gentle movement. Option A is too restrictive. Option B stresses inflamed joints. Option D is contraindicated during active inflammation.

questionText: A 41-year-old patient reports clicking in the shoulder with overhead activities. Examination reveals positive O'Brien's test, biceps load test II, and pain at 90-120 degrees of abduction. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Superior labral anterior-posterior (SLAP) lesion
options.C: Acromioclavicular joint arthritis
options.D: Subacromial impingement

explanation: Option B is correct. SLAP lesions present with clicking, positive O'Brien's test (pain with resisted forward flexion in internal rotation that decreases with external rotation), positive biceps load test, and pain in mid-range abduction. These tests are specific for superior labral pathology. Option A would show weakness and positive impingement signs. Option C causes pain localized to AC joint with cross-body adduction. Option D shows painful arc and impingement signs without positive SLAP-specific tests.

questionText: A 67-year-old patient with Parkinson's disease demonstrates festinating gait and multiple near-falls. Berg Balance Scale is 38/56. What intervention has the STRONGEST evidence for fall reduction?

options.B: Ankle strengthening exercises
options.C: Virtual reality gaming for balance
options.D: Task-specific balance training with external cueing and progressive challenges

explanation: Option D is correct. Task-specific balance training addressing PD-specific deficits (weight shifting, stepping strategies, dual-task training) combined with external cueing (visual, auditory) has strongest evidence for reducing falls in PD. Exercises should be progressive and challenging. BBS 38/56 indicates moderate fall risk requiring intensive intervention. Option A addresses vestibular, not PD-specific, deficits. Option B is too limited. Option C is emerging but not yet strongest evidence.

questionText: A 59-year-old patient in Phase II cardiac rehabilitation suddenly develops chest pain, diaphoresis, and nausea during exercise. HR is 142 bpm, BP is 86/54 mmHg. What is the MOST appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Stop exercise immediately, activate emergency response system, administer oxygen, place patient supine with legs elevated, and monitor closely
options.B: Reduce exercise intensity by 50% and continue monitoring
options.C: Have patient sit upright and perform deep breathing exercises
options.D: Administer nitroglycerin and continue with reduced intensity exercise

explanation: Option A is correct. This presentation suggests acute myocardial infarction or cardiogenic shock (chest pain, diaphoresis, nausea, hypotension). Immediate actions: stop all activity, activate EMS, administer oxygen, position for hypotension management (supine with legs elevated if no pulmonary edema), monitor vitals and ECG if available, and be prepared for CPR. This is a medical emergency. Options B, C, and D all inappropriately continue or delay emergency response.

questionText: A 55-year-old patient with a neuropathic ulcer on the plantar first metatarsal head has been non-weight-bearing in a total contact cast for 6 weeks. The wound is now closed. What is the MOST important next step?

options.A: Immediate return to regular footwear and full activity
options.B: Continue total contact cast for 3 more months
options.C: Gradual return to weight-bearing in accommodative/therapeutic footwear with pressure redistribution insoles and ongoing monitoring
options.D: Surgical intervention to prevent recurrence

explanation: Option C is correct. After neuropathic ulcer closure, gradual return to activity with pressure redistribution is essential. Therapeutic footwear, custom insoles, patient education on daily foot inspection, and ongoing monitoring prevent recurrence (which occurs in 40% within 1 year). Biomechanical abnormalities and neuropathy persist. Option A leads to rapid recurrence. Option B is too conservative once healed. Option D may be needed for severe deformity but isn't automatic first step.

questionText: A 29-year-old patient 16 weeks pregnant reports sacroiliac joint pain rated 6/10 that limits walking. The patient asks about manual therapy. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: All manual therapy is contraindicated during pregnancy
options.B: Gentle muscle energy techniques and soft tissue mobilization are safe; high-velocity thrust techniques should be avoided or used with caution
options.C: High-velocity manipulation is the only effective treatment for SI pain
options.D: Manual therapy should be delayed until after delivery

explanation: Option B is correct. Gentle manual therapy techniques including muscle energy, soft tissue mobilization, and gentle joint mobilizations are generally safe during pregnancy and can be effective for SI joint pain. High-velocity thrust techniques are controversial; many therapists avoid them or use extreme caution with modified techniques and positioning. Option A is too restrictive. Option C is inappropriate for pregnancy. Option D unnecessarily prolongs suffering when safe effective treatment exists.

questionText: A 2-year-old child demonstrates toe-walking, tight heel cords, positive Babinski reflex, and hypertonia in lower extremities. Upper extremities show normal tone and movement. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Spina bifida
options.C: Cerebral palsy, spastic diplegia
options.D: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease

explanation: Option C is correct. Spastic diplegia (cerebral palsy) presents with greater involvement of lower than upper extremities, toe-walking, tight heel cords, positive Babinski (upper motor neuron sign), and hypertonia. This is the most common form of CP. Option A shows progressive proximal weakness without spasticity or positive Babinski. Option B would show lower motor neuron signs and sensory deficits based on lesion level. Option D is peripheral neuropathy with distal weakness, not spasticity.

questionText: A 73-year-old patient with pulmonary fibrosis uses 3 L/min oxygen continuously. The patient wants to increase activity level. What parameter should be PRIMARILY monitored during activity progression?

options.B: Oxygen saturation and dyspnea level
options.C: Blood pressure changes
options.D: Respiratory rate alone

explanation: Option B is correct. Pulmonary fibrosis patients are limited by gas exchange impairment and dyspnea rather than cardiovascular response. SpO2 monitoring is critical; oxygen supplementation should be adjusted to maintain SpO2 ≥88-90%. Dyspnea assessment (Borg scale) guides intensity. These parameters are primary limiters to activity. While all vital signs should be monitored (Options A, C, D), SpO2 and dyspnea are the primary concerns in interstitial lung disease.

questionText: A 38-year-old patient with systemic lupus erythematosus reports fatigue, joint pain, and photosensitivity. Lab results show positive ANA and low complement levels. What exercise modification is MOST important?

options.B: Outdoor exercise for vitamin D production
options.C: No exercise during disease flares or remission
options.D: Moderate intensity exercise avoiding UV exposure, with modifications during flares

explanation: Option D is correct. SLE patients benefit from regular moderate exercise but require special considerations: avoid direct sunlight due to photosensitivity, modify intensity during flares, monitor for exercise-induced flares, and balance activity with rest due to fatigue. Exercise improves cardiovascular health and function without worsening disease. Option A is too intense and may trigger flares. Option B exposes patient to UV triggering photosensitivity. Option C is too restrictive; gentle exercise during remission is beneficial.

questionText: A 56-year-old patient underwent lumbar laminectomy at L4-L5 three weeks ago. The patient reports new onset of sharp radiating pain down the right leg and numbness in the L5 distribution. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.A: Immediately contact the surgeon regarding new neurological symptoms
options.B: Continue with current exercise program as radicular symptoms are expected post-operatively
options.C: Add neural mobilization exercises to address the nerve irritation
options.D: Increase lumbar flexion exercises to open the neural foramen

explanation: Option A is correct. New or worsening neurological symptoms following spine surgery could indicate serious complications: epidural hematoma, recurrent disc herniation, infection, hardware failure, or surgical site issues. Immediate physician communication is mandatory. This is a red flag requiring medical evaluation. Options B, C, and D all inappropriately continue therapy when medical emergency may exist. New post-operative neurological deficits are NEVER normal and always require immediate physician consultation.

questionText: A 55-year-old patient with multiple sclerosis reports increased spasticity interfering with transfers and ADLs. The patient takes baclofen daily. What intervention would be MOST beneficial as adjunct to medication?

options.A: Aggressive passive stretching into pain range to reduce spasticity
options.B: Application of heat before activities to reduce tone
options.C: Prolonged static stretching, positioning program, and functional task practice
options.D: High-resistance strengthening exercises

explanation: Option C is correct. Spasticity management in MS includes prolonged static stretching (30-60 seconds), positioning to prevent contractures, ROM exercises, and functional task practice. These complement pharmacological management. Option A (aggressive painful stretching) can trigger increased spasticity and is contraindicated. Option B (heat) may worsen MS symptoms through Uhthoff's phenomenon; cold is typically better tolerated. Option D may increase spasticity through excessive effort.

questionText: A 62-year-old patient post-COVID-19 reports severe fatigue and exercise intolerance. Evaluation shows resting tachycardia (HR 98 bpm) and orthostatic hypotension (BP drops from 128/78 to 98/64 upon standing). What does this suggest?

options.B: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) or dysautonomia
options.C: Deconditioning only
options.D: Chronic heart failure

explanation: Option B is correct. Post-COVID POTS/dysautonomia is increasingly recognized, characterized by orthostatic intolerance, tachycardia, fatigue, and exercise intolerance due to autonomic dysfunction. Orthostatic hypotension with resting tachycardia suggests autonomic involvement beyond simple deconditioning. This requires specific management: gradual reconditioning, increased fluid/salt intake, compression garments, and possibly medical management. Options A and C underestimate complexity. Option D doesn't explain orthostatic symptoms.

questionText: A 42-year-old office worker presents with 3-month history of bilateral wrist and hand pain, worse at night. Examination reveals positive Phalen's test bilaterally at 30 seconds, positive Tinel's sign at both wrists, and decreased grip strength (R: 45 lbs, L: 42 lbs; normal for gender/age: 70-80 lbs). Two-point discrimination is 7mm on both thumbs. EMG confirms bilateral moderate carpal tunnel syndrome. The patient has tried night splints for 4 weeks with minimal improvement. What is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: Continue conservative treatment for another 8 weeks before considering other options
options.B: Immediately refer for bilateral surgical release
options.C: Trial of corticosteroid injections combined with ergonomic workplace modifications and continued splinting
options.D: Aggressive neural mobilization exercises with median nerve tensioning

explanation: Option C is correct. Moderate CTS with 4 weeks of failed conservative treatment warrants escalation. Corticosteroid injection provides effective short-to-intermediate term relief (evidence shows 70-80% improvement at 1-3 months) and can be combined with workplace ergonomic modifications addressing the occupational component. This is appropriate before surgery and more aggressive than current treatment. Option A is too conservative given moderate severity and 3-month symptom duration. Option B is premature; injections should be tried before surgery in moderate cases without severe atrophy. Option D could exacerbate symptoms in moderate CTS.

questionText: A 68-year-old patient is admitted to acute care following right MCA stroke 24 hours ago. Current presentation: left hemiplegia with flaccid tone, unable to follow commands consistently, NIHSS score of 16. Blood pressure is 165/95 mmHg. The physician orders physical therapy evaluation. What is the MOST appropriate initial intervention?

options.A: Aggressive mobilization including sitting at edge of bed and standing
options.B: Positioning program, passive ROM, and assessment of arousal/cognition; defer aggressive mobility given BP and acute presentation
options.C: No intervention for 72 hours to allow neurological stabilization
options.D: Constraint-induced movement therapy for the affected upper extremity

explanation: Option B is correct. In hyperacute stroke (<24-48 hours), especially with NIHSS >15 (severe stroke) and elevated BP, initial therapy focuses on positioning to prevent complications (pressure injuries, contractures, aspiration), gentle passive ROM, and assessment. Aggressive mobilization may be delayed 24-48 hours given severity and BP instability. Current guidelines support very early mobilization in stable patients, but this patient's severity and BP warrant caution. Option A is too aggressive for <24 hours with NIHSS 16 and elevated BP. Option C is too conservative; positioning and PROM should begin immediately. Option D is inappropriate for acute flaccid hemiplegia.

questionText: A 71-year-old patient in ICU is day 5 post-CABG × 3. Chest tubes were removed yesterday. Current vitals: HR 88 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, SpO2 96% on 2L O2, RR 18. Sternal incision shows slight serosanguineous drainage but edges are approximated. The patient is alert and cooperative. What is the MOST appropriate mobility progression?

options.A: Bed rest only until drainage completely resolves
options.B: Supine exercises only, avoiding all trunk movement
options.C: Progress to sitting edge of bed, standing, and ambulation with sternal precautions
options.D: Full upper extremity resistance exercises to prevent muscle loss

explanation: Option C is correct. Day 5 post-CABG with stable vitals and chest tubes removed indicates readiness for mobility progression. Sitting, standing, and ambulation are appropriate with sternal precautions (no pushing/pulling >5-10 lbs, no reaching behind back, supporting incision with pillow for cough). Slight serosanguineous drainage at day 5 is not uncommon if edges are approximated. Early mobilization reduces complications and hospital length of stay. Option A is unnecessarily restrictive. Option B doesn't optimize functional recovery. Option D violates sternal precautions (typically 6-8 weeks for UE resistance).

questionText: A 55-year-old patient with diabetes and ESRD on hemodialysis presents with a Wagner Grade 2 ulcer on the plantar surface of the right foot (2cm × 1.5cm, extending to tendon). ABI cannot be obtained due to calcified vessels. Pedal pulses are non-palpable by palpation but present by Doppler. Toe pressure is 35 mmHg. What does the toe pressure indicate about wound healing potential?

options.A: Excellent healing potential; proceed with standard wound care
options.B: Adequate perfusion; compression therapy is indicated
options.C: Borderline perfusion; healing is possible with optimal wound care
options.D: Poor perfusion; wound healing is unlikely without revascularization

explanation: Option D is correct. Toe pressure <30 mmHg indicates critical limb ischemia, and 30-40 mmHg suggests severe PAD with poor healing potential. This patient at 35 mmHg has inadequate perfusion for wound healing. Vascular surgery consultation for revascularization evaluation is essential. ABI unreliable due to vessel calcification (common in diabetes and ESRD), making toe pressures more accurate. Option A ignores critical perfusion deficit. Option B is dangerous; compression is contraindicated with arterial disease. Option C is too optimistic; at 35 mmHg, healing without revascularization is very unlikely.

questionText: A 28-year-old soccer player sustained a grade 2 MCL sprain 6 weeks ago. Current examination shows full ROM, 4+/5 quadriceps strength, negative valgus stress test at 0 degrees, but 5mm opening with valgus stress at 30 degrees (compared to 2mm on uninvolved side). Single-leg hop test shows 85% of uninvolved limb. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation regarding return to sport?

options.A: Full return to competitive soccer immediately
options.B: Progress to sport-specific training with functional brace; delay full competition until hop test >90% and improved stability
options.C: No return to sport; refer for surgical reconstruction due to residual laxity
options.D: Return to practice only, avoid games indefinitely

explanation: Option B is correct. At 6 weeks post-Grade 2 MCL with good ROM and strength but residual laxity (5mm vs 2mm) and suboptimal hop test (85%), the athlete needs continued rehabilitation before full sport return. Functional bracing provides external stability during sport-specific progression. Return-to-sport criteria typically include: >90% on hop testing, isokinetic strength >85-90%, and functional testing. Current status is close but not quite meeting criteria. Option A is premature given objective deficits. Option C is excessive; isolated Grade 2 MCL rarely requires surgery with proper rehab. Option D is unnecessarily restrictive.

questionText: A 4-year-old child with spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy (GMFCS Level IV) has developed a 25-degree lumbar scoliosis. The family asks about treatment options. What is the MOST evidence-based recommendation?

options.A: Immediate surgical spinal fusion to prevent progression
options.B: Aggressive stretching program to correct the curvature
options.C: Positioning program, adaptive seating with lateral supports, monitoring for progression; bracing has limited evidence in neuromuscular scoliosis
options.D: No intervention as scoliosis does not affect function in CP

explanation: Option C is correct. Neuromuscular scoliosis in CP is managed differently than idiopathic scoliosis. At 25 degrees in a 4-year-old GMFCS IV, initial management focuses on positioning, adaptive seating with lateral trunk supports, and monitoring. Bracing has limited evidence for preventing progression in neuromuscular scoliosis (unlike idiopathic). Surgery is typically considered for curves >40-50 degrees or when affecting sitting balance/respiratory function. Option A is premature for 25 degrees. Option B cannot correct structural curves. Option D ignores impact on sitting, positioning, and potential respiratory effects as curve progresses.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) is beginning a walking program. Resting vitals: HR 78 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, SpO2 93%. During 6-minute walk test, patient completes 320 meters with SpO2 dropping to 86%, HR 128 bpm, and reports severe dyspnea (Borg 7/10). What is the MOST important exercise modification?

options.A: No exercise due to PAH diagnosis
options.B: Continue walking program without modifications as desaturation is expected
options.C: High-intensity interval training to improve cardiovascular capacity
options.D: Supervised low-to-moderate intensity exercise with supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥88-90% and frequent rest periods

explanation: Option D is correct. PAH patients can safely participate in supervised exercise with appropriate modifications. Key considerations: maintain SpO2 ≥88-90% with supplemental oxygen, use low-to-moderate intensity (Borg 3-5/10), include frequent rest periods, and monitor for signs of right heart failure. The 320m 6MWT distance suggests functional limitation, and desaturation to 86% requires oxygen supplementation. Exercise improves functional capacity and quality of life in PAH when properly monitored. Option A is outdated; exercise is beneficial. Option B ignores dangerous desaturation. Option C is contraindicated; high intensity can precipitate right heart failure.

questionText: A 62-year-old patient with polymyositis reports progressive proximal muscle weakness over 3 months. Lab work shows elevated CK (3,500 U/L; normal <200) and positive anti-Jo-1 antibodies. The patient is starting prednisone and asks about exercise. What is the MOST appropriate exercise recommendation during active disease?

options.A: High-resistance strength training to counteract muscle weakness
options.B: Gentle active ROM and low-intensity activities; avoid resistance exercise until disease is controlled and CK normalizes
options.C: Complete bed rest until CK levels normalize
options.D: Moderate-intensity eccentric exercises to build strength

explanation: Option B is correct. During active inflammatory myopathy (elevated CK, progressive weakness), resistance exercise can worsen muscle inflammation and damage. Management during active phase: gentle active ROM, functional activities, avoid resistance training until disease controlled (CK normalizing, weakness stabilizing). Once inflammation controlled, gradual progressive resistance can be safely introduced. Option A would exacerbate inflammation. Option C causes unnecessary deconditioning; gentle movement is safe and maintains function. Option D (eccentric exercise) creates greatest muscle damage and is especially contraindicated.

questionText: A 35-year-old CrossFit athlete presents with insidious anterior hip/groin pain over 6 months, worse with squatting and running. Examination reveals limited hip internal rotation (15 degrees; normal 30-40), positive FADIR test, and anterosuperior labral tear confirmed on MRI with mild cam-type femoroacetabular impingement. Conservative treatment for 12 weeks has provided minimal relief. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Continue conservative treatment for 6 more months
options.B: Stop all athletic activity permanently due to labral pathology
options.C: Referral to orthopedic surgeon for arthroscopic evaluation; may require labral repair and cam decompression
options.D: Aggressive hip flexor strengthening program

explanation: Option C is correct. Symptomatic labral tear with FAI in young active individual who has failed 12 weeks of conservative treatment (activity modification, PT, NSAIDs) is indication for surgical consultation. Hip arthroscopy with labral repair and cam decompression has good outcomes in appropriate candidates. Continued conservative treatment (Option A) unlikely to improve with structural abnormality and failed trial. Option B is unnecessarily pessimistic; surgery can allow return to sport. Option D doesn't address structural problem and may worsen impingement.

questionText: A 76-year-old patient presents to home health following hospitalization for community-acquired pneumonia. Medical history includes moderate Alzheimer's disease. During initial evaluation, the patient scores 18/30 on MMSE, demonstrates impaired judgment, and becomes agitated during transfers. Spouse reports patient was ambulating independently pre-hospitalization. What is the MOST appropriate intervention approach?

options.A: Task-specific functional training using simple one-step commands, consistent routine, environmental simplification, and caregiver training
options.B: Cognitive rehabilitation exercises to improve memory and attention
options.C: Detailed verbal explanations of all activities to improve understanding
options.D: Defer all therapy until patient is less agitated

explanation: Option A is correct. Dementia rehabilitation requires task-specific functional approach with environmental modifications: simple one-step instructions, consistent routine/timing, reduced stimulation, familiar environment, demonstration over explanation, and extensive caregiver training. Focus on recovering pre-morbid function using procedural memory. Option B is ineffective for moderate-stage Alzheimer's; focus should be functional. Option C overwhelms patient with dementia; simple commands are more effective. Option D prevents recovery of functional mobility; strategies can manage agitation.

questionText: A 64-year-old patient with bronchiectasis presents with chronic productive cough, daily purulent sputum (30-50 mL), and recurrent infections. CT shows bilateral cylindrical bronchiectasis. PFTs show mild obstruction. What airway clearance technique is MOST evidence-based for this patient?

options.B: Incentive spirometry three times daily
options.C: High-frequency chest wall oscillation or PEP therapy combined with airway clearance techniques
options.D: Coughing exercises only

explanation: Option C is correct. Bronchiectasis requires aggressive airway clearance due to impaired mucociliary clearance and thick secretions. Evidence supports oscillatory PEP devices (Acapella, Flutter) or high-frequency chest wall oscillation combined with positioning and effective cough techniques. Multiple sessions daily are typically needed. This combination mobilizes secretions from peripheral airways and facilitates expectoration. Option A is insufficient alone for volume of secretions. Option B doesn't mobilize secretions. Option D alone is insufficient; secretions must first be mobilized from peripheral airways.

questionText: A 72-year-old patient with Stage 3 pressure injury over the sacrum has been treated with appropriate wound care and pressure relief for 3 weeks. Initial wound size was 4cm × 3cm × 2cm deep. Current measurements: 3.8cm × 2.9cm × 2cm with 60% granulation, 40% slough. What does this wound status indicate?

options.A: Excellent healing trajectory; continue current treatment
options.B: Suboptimal healing; requires reassessment of treatment plan and possible debridement of slough
options.C: Normal healing for Stage 3 injury; no changes needed
options.D: Wound deterioration requiring immediate surgical intervention

explanation: Option B is correct. After 3 weeks, expected healing shows: significant reduction in wound size (this shows minimal change: 4×3 to 3.8×2.9cm), increasing granulation tissue (only 60%), and decreasing necrotic tissue. Persistent 40% slough at 3 weeks suggests inadequate debridement or underlying issues (infection, poor perfusion, inadequate nutrition, unrelieved pressure). Reassessment needed: wound culture if infection suspected, nutrition status, pressure relief adequacy, consider sharp debridement of slough. Option A ignores minimal progress. Option C accepts inadequate healing. Option D is premature; optimization of conservative measures should precede surgery.

questionText: A 52-year-old violinist presents with 8-month history of lateral elbow pain rated 6/10, worse with gripping and extending wrist. Previous treatment includes rest, ice, NSAIDs, counterforce bracing, and 6 weeks of physical therapy with limited improvement. Examination confirms lateral epicondylalgia. What intervention has the STRONGEST evidence for chronic tendinopathy at this stage?

options.A: Corticosteroid injection for long-term pain relief
options.B: Complete immobilization in a wrist splint
options.C: Ultrasound therapy at therapeutic dosage
options.D: Eccentric exercise program (Tyler Twist protocol) or platelet-rich plasma injection

explanation: Option D is correct. Chronic lateral epicondylalgia (>6 months) with failed conservative treatment requires advanced interventions. Strongest evidence: eccentric exercise (Tyler Twist or similar protocols) promotes tendon remodeling and has superior long-term outcomes. Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injections show promise for chronic cases in recent literature. Option A provides short-term relief but poor long-term outcomes and may impair healing. Option B causes stiffness and muscle atrophy without addressing pathology. Option C lacks strong evidence for efficacy in tendinopathy.

questionText: A 28-year-old patient sustained complete T6 spinal cord injury 8 weeks ago. The patient is independent in wheelchair mobility on level surfaces but reports severe fatigue and lightheadedness when sitting upright for >15 minutes. BP supine is 118/76 mmHg; sitting BP is 88/52 mmHg with HR increase from 68 to 94 bpm. What is the MOST appropriate intervention?

options.A: Restrict all sitting activities until cardiovascular system adapts
options.B: Increase fluid intake only
options.C: Progressive sitting tolerance training with abdominal binder, compression stockings, and gradual position changes; consider midodrine if symptoms persist
options.D: Recommend surgical intervention for cardiovascular dysfunction

explanation: Option C is correct. Orthostatic hypotension is common in SCI above T6 due to loss of sympathetic vascular control. Management: abdominal binder and compression stockings (increase venous return), gradual position changes, progressive sitting tolerance, adequate hydration/salt, elevation of head during sleep. Pharmacological intervention (midodrine, fludrocortisone) if conservative measures inadequate. This is managed conservatively with reconditioning. Option A prevents necessary adaptation. Option B alone is insufficient. Option D is not indicated; this is physiological adaptation issue, not structural cardiovascular problem.

questionText: A 69-year-old patient with chronic systolic heart failure (EF 28%) is in Phase II cardiac rehabilitation. During today's session, patient reports 2-kg weight gain over 3 days, increased dyspnea, and bilateral ankle edema. Vitals: HR 102 bpm, BP 142/88 mmHg (baseline 118/74), SpO2 91% on room air. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.A: Do not exercise; immediately notify physician of signs of decompensated heart failure
options.B: Proceed with exercise at 50% usual intensity
options.C: Perform exercise but in supine position to reduce cardiac demand
options.D: Continue normal exercise program as symptoms are mild

explanation: Option A is correct. This patient shows classic signs of acute decompensated heart failure: rapid weight gain (fluid retention), increased dyspnea, peripheral edema, tachycardia, elevated BP (from baseline), and decreased SpO2. Exercise is contraindicated during acute decompensation and could precipitate further deterioration, pulmonary edema, or arrhythmias. Immediate physician notification for medication adjustment (likely diuretics, possible hospitalization) is required. Options B, C, and D all inappropriately continue exercise during acute cardiac decompensation, which is dangerous.

questionText: A 45-year-old patient with end-stage renal disease has been on hemodialysis for 2 years. The patient asks about exercise on dialysis days. Current labs show hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL (normal 12-16), serum creatinine 8.5 mg/dL. What is the MOST appropriate exercise recommendation?

options.A: No exercise on dialysis days or for 24 hours after dialysis
options.B: Light-to-moderate exercise on non-dialysis days; gentle exercise during first 2 hours of dialysis may be beneficial
options.C: High-intensity exercise immediately after dialysis when fluid balance is optimal
options.D: Exercise only during the last hour of dialysis treatment

explanation: Option B is correct. ESRD patients benefit from exercise but require special timing considerations. Evidence supports: light-to-moderate exercise on non-dialysis days, or gentle exercise (cycling, resistance bands) during the first 2 hours of dialysis session (improves dialysis adequacy and is well-tolerated). Avoid vigorous exercise for 4-6 hours post-dialysis due to hemodynamic instability, fluid shifts, and fatigue. The anemia (Hb 9.2) requires intensity modification but doesn't contraindicate exercise. Option A is too restrictive. Option C risks hypotension and fatigue immediately post-dialysis. Option D during final hour risks post-dialysis hypotension.

questionText: A 24-year-old rugby player sustained a shoulder dislocation (anterior) 3 weeks ago, reduced in emergency department. MRI shows Bankart lesion and Hill-Sachs defect. The patient has regained 140 degrees flexion and 110 degrees abduction, with 4/5 rotator cuff strength. Patient wants to return to rugby. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Full return to rugby with no restrictions
options.B: Aggressive stretching to achieve full ROM before any strengthening
options.C: Return to rugby in a shoulder stabilization brace only
options.D: Referral to orthopedic surgeon for surgical stabilization discussion given high recurrence risk in young contact athlete with structural damage

explanation: Option D is correct. Young athletes (<25 years) in contact sports with first-time traumatic anterior dislocation and structural damage (Bankart lesion, Hill-Sachs) have 70-90% recurrence risk with conservative treatment. Arthroscopic stabilization significantly reduces recurrence (10-15%) and allows safer return to contact sports. Given age, sport demands, and structural pathology, surgical consultation is warranted to discuss risks/benefits. Options A and C accept unacceptably high redislocation risk. Option B focuses on wrong priority; ROM is adequate, stability is the issue.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with diabetic peripheral neuropathy reports burning pain in both feet rated 7/10, worse at night, significantly limiting walking tolerance. Neurological exam shows absent ankle reflexes, decreased vibration sense, and 10g monofilament insensitivity. The patient is on gabapentin with partial relief. What physical therapy intervention has the BEST evidence for neuropathic pain reduction?

options.A: Hot packs to increase circulation to the feet
options.B: Aggressive sensory reeducation with various textures
options.C: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) or infrared light therapy combined with exercise
options.D: Complete avoidance of weight-bearing activities

explanation: Option C is correct. For diabetic peripheral neuropathy pain: TENS (gate control theory for pain modulation) and infrared/monochromatic infrared energy (MIRE) therapy show evidence for pain reduction. Combined with exercise program (improves glycemic control, nerve function), this provides multimodal approach. Exercise itself improves neuropathic symptoms. Option A is contraindicated; impaired sensation creates burn risk. Option B is more appropriate for hypersensitivity, not loss of protective sensation with pain. Option D prevents important benefits of exercise and leads to deconditioning.

questionText: A 48-year-old patient with obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS) uses BiPAP nocturnally. ABG on room air shows: pH 7.36, PaCO2 52 mmHg (elevated), PaO2 68 mmHg, HCO3 28 mEq/L (elevated). The patient wants to start exercising. What is the MOST important consideration?

options.A: Exercise is contraindicated with elevated CO2
options.B: Only aquatic exercise to reduce weight-bearing stress
options.C: High-intensity exercise to maximize weight loss
options.D: Supervised exercise with close monitoring of SpO2 and symptoms; supplemental oxygen if needed; focus on gradual progression and weight loss

explanation: Option D is correct. OHS patients have chronic hypercapnia and hypoxemia due to obesity-related ventilatory restriction. They can exercise safely with: careful monitoring (SpO2, dyspnea), supplemental oxygen as needed, gradual progression starting low-intensity, and weight loss focus (which improves underlying condition). The compensated respiratory acidosis (pH 7.36 with elevated CO2 and HCO3) indicates chronic stable condition. Exercise is beneficial for weight loss and improving respiratory function. Option A is too restrictive. Option B is unnecessarily limiting. Option C is too aggressive for deconditioned patient with respiratory compromise.

questionText: A 66-year-old patient with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate and prednisone develops a small skin tear (2cm) on the forearm from minor trauma. The wound edges are well-approximated but surrounding skin is thin and fragile. What is the MOST appropriate initial wound management?

options.B: Gentle approximation of skin flap, non-adherent dressing, protection from further trauma; monitor for infection due to immunosuppression
options.C: Immediate suturing of the wound
options.D: Silver sulfadiazine cream and occlusive dressing

explanation: Option B is correct. Skin tears in patients on corticosteroids (thin fragile skin) and immunosuppressive therapy require gentle handling: approximate skin flap if viable, non-adherent dressing to prevent further trauma, protection from shear/friction, and vigilant infection monitoring (immunosuppressed state increases risk). Avoid tape on fragile skin; use wrap or tubular bandage. Option A would remove viable tissue and enlarge wound unnecessarily. Option C risks further tissue damage and isn't indicated for well-approximated skin tear. Option D may not be necessary initially; focus on protection and approximation.

questionText: A 59-year-old patient presents with gradual onset of anterior knee pain over 6 months. Examination reveals pain with patellar compression, crepitus, moderate effusion, and decreased quadriceps strength (4-/5). Radiographs show Grade 3 patellofemoral osteoarthritis with joint space narrowing and osteophytes. What intervention combination is MOST evidence-based?

options.A: Complete rest and avoidance of all knee flexion activities
options.B: Open kinetic chain knee extension exercises (0-45 degrees) for quadriceps strengthening
options.C: Closed kinetic chain exercises, quadriceps strengthening emphasizing VMO, patellar mobilizations, activity modification
options.D: Immediate referral for total knee arthroplasty

explanation: Option C is correct. Patellofemoral OA evidence-based treatment: closed kinetic chain exercises (reduce patellofemoral compressive forces), VMO strengthening (improves patellar tracking), patellar mobilizations (address soft tissue restrictions), activity modification (avoid excessive loading), weight management if applicable, and pain modulation. Conservative management is first-line. Option A leads to muscle atrophy and stiffness. Option B (open chain 0-45°) creates excessive patellofemoral compression. Option D is premature; extensive conservative treatment should precede surgery discussion.

questionText: A 12-year-old child with Duchenne muscular dystrophy is losing ambulation. The child can still walk 50 meters with bilateral ankle-foot orthoses but falls frequently. Pulmonary function tests show FVC 45% predicted. What is the MOST critical intervention priority at this stage?

options.B: Aquatic therapy for cardiovascular conditioning
options.C: Stretching program only to maintain flexibility
options.D: Power wheelchair prescription, respiratory muscle training, and monitoring for scoliosis/respiratory decline; gentle ROM

explanation: Option D is correct. Late ambulatory/early non-ambulatory DMD with declining pulmonary function (FVC 45%) requires: power wheelchair for mobility/energy conservation, respiratory assessment/interventions (breathing exercises, airway clearance, monitoring for nocturnal hypoventilation), scoliosis monitoring (accelerates with loss of ambulation), and gentle ROM (avoid overwork). Quality of life and respiratory function preservation are priorities over forcing continued ambulation. Option A risks overwork weakness and exhaustion. Option B may be too demanding given cardiopulmonary decline. Option C addresses only one aspect.

questionText: A 54-year-old patient underwent bilateral lung transplant 6 months ago for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Current immunosuppression: tacrolimus, mycophenolate, prednisone. Recent labs show: WBC 3,200/μL (low), creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (elevated from baseline 1.0). The patient reports fatigue but wants to increase exercise. What is the MOST important precaution?

options.A: No exercise restrictions; patient is fully recovered at 6 months
options.B: Only outdoor exercise for fresh air and vitamin D
options.C: Strict infection control (avoid sick contacts, hand hygiene, mask in public spaces); monitor for signs of rejection; adjust intensity for fatigue
options.D: High-intensity training to overcome immunosuppression side effects

explanation: Option C is correct. Lung transplant patients on immunosuppression require vigilant infection control (leading cause of mortality). Low WBC (3,200) increases infection susceptibility. Additional concerns: monitor for rejection signs (declining PFTs, dyspnea, fatigue), medication side effects (elevated creatinine suggests tacrolimus nephrotoxicity), and appropriate exercise progression considering fatigue and labs. Exercise is beneficial but must be balanced with safety. Option A ignores immunosuppression risks. Option B increases exposure risk. Option D is excessive given labs and fatigue.

questionText: A 36-year-old pregnant patient at 34 weeks gestation with gestational diabetes (controlled with insulin) asks about exercise. Current blood glucose log shows fasting 88-95 mg/dL, 1-hour post-meal 130-145 mg/dL. Blood pressure is 128/82 mmHg. What exercise recommendation is MOST appropriate?

options.A: No exercise due to insulin requirement
options.B: Moderate-intensity aerobic exercise (walking, swimming, stationary cycling) most days, monitoring blood glucose before/after; avoid supine positions
options.C: High-intensity exercise to maximize glucose control
options.D: Resistance training only to build strength for labor

explanation: Option B is correct. Gestational diabetes with good glucose control benefits from continued moderate exercise: walking, swimming, stationary cycling 20-30 minutes most days. Monitor glucose to prevent hypoglycemia (carry glucose tablets), adequate hydration, avoid supine position after 20 weeks, warning sign education (vaginal bleeding, decreased fetal movement, contractions). Exercise improves glucose control and reduces complications. Option A is too restrictive; exercise is beneficial. Option C may cause hypoglycemia or excessive stress. Option D alone doesn't optimize glucose control.

questionText: A 67-year-old patient underwent reverse total shoulder arthroplasty 8 weeks ago for massive rotator cuff tear arthropathy. Current ROM: passive forward flexion 135 degrees, active 120 degrees, passive abduction 110 degrees, active 95 degrees. External rotation: passive 30 degrees, active 20 degrees. What is the MOST appropriate intervention focus?

options.A: Aggressive passive stretching to achieve full ROM
options.B: Strengthening of rotator cuff muscles
options.C: Deltoid strengthening and active ROM exercises within available range; avoid excessive external rotation stretching
options.D: Complete restriction of active motion for 3 more months

explanation: Option C is correct. Reverse TSA relies on deltoid for motion (rotator cuff deficient/absent). At 8 weeks: progressive deltoid strengthening, active ROM within available range, functional training. External rotation is intentionally limited in reverse TSA; excessive ER stretching can compromise stability. Current ROM is appropriate for 8 weeks. Option A risks prosthesis instability, especially forced ER. Option B is futile/harmful; rotator cuff doesn't exist or function (hence reverse TSA). Option D is too restrictive; active motion typically progresses 6-8 weeks post-op.

questionText: A 43-year-old patient presents with 4-week history of progressive bilateral foot drop, absent ankle reflexes, and distal leg weakness. Sensation is intact. EMG shows acute denervation in tibialis anterior and peroneus longus bilaterally. ESR and CRP are normal. What is the MOST likely diagnosis requiring immediate referral?

options.B: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (Guillain-Barré syndrome variant)
options.C: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
options.D: Diabetic neuropathy

explanation: Option B is correct. Acute (4 weeks) progressive bilateral symmetric weakness with areflexia and EMG denervation suggests acute inflammatory polyneuropathy (Guillain-Barré or variant). Some GBS variants present with distal weakness. The rapidity, symmetry, areflexia, and EMG findings distinguish this from other options. Immediate medical referral critical for: diagnosis confirmation (LP showing elevated protein), monitoring respiratory function, possible immunotherapy (IVIG or plasmapheresis). Option A unlikely to be bilateral and simultaneous. Option C is gradual, has upper motor neuron signs. Option D develops slowly, has sensory involvement.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with Class III heart failure (NYHA) on optimal medical management has been performing home exercise (walking 20 min/day, 5 days/week) for 3 months with good tolerance. The patient wants to add resistance training. What resistance training prescription is MOST appropriate?

options.A: No resistance training; focus only on aerobic exercise
options.B: Light-to-moderate resistance (40-50% 1RM), 10-15 repetitions, major muscle groups, 2 days/week, avoiding Valsalva
options.C: High-intensity resistance training (>80% 1RM) to maximize strength gains
options.D: Isometric exercises only to avoid cardiovascular stress

explanation: Option B is correct. Class III HF patients stable on optimal medical management can safely perform resistance training: light-to-moderate intensity (40-50% 1RM), higher repetitions (10-15), major muscle groups, 2-3 days/week, avoid Valsalva maneuver, progress gradually. Improves strength, functional capacity, quality of life without adverse cardiac events. Option A is outdated; resistance training is now recommended. Option C creates excessive BP response and myocardial oxygen demand. Option D is actually higher BP response than dynamic resistance; isometric is not preferred.

questionText: A 54-year-old patient with chronic venous insufficiency develops acute onset of increased leg edema, warmth, erythema, and pain in the left calf over 24 hours. The patient has been compliant with compression therapy. Homan's sign is positive. What is the MOST appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Immediate physician referral for deep vein thrombosis evaluation; discontinue compression until DVT ruled out
options.B: Increase compression to 40-50 mmHg to reduce edema
options.C: Apply heat to reduce pain and inflammation
options.D: Continue current treatment as symptoms are expected with venous insufficiency

explanation: Option A is correct. Acute unilateral leg pain, swelling, warmth, and erythema in patient with venous disease raises high suspicion for DVT (Homan's sign has low sensitivity/specificity but positive finding with symptoms is concerning). This is medical emergency requiring: immediate physician referral, likely Doppler ultrasound, possible anticoagulation. Discontinue compression until DVT excluded (compression contraindicated with acute DVT). Option B could dislodge clot or worsen acute thrombosis. Option C increases risk if DVT present. Option D misses potentially life-threatening condition.

questionText: A 31-year-old postpartum patient (6 weeks post-delivery) reports severe pelvic pain, inability to walk without significant pain, and audible clicking from the symphysis pubis. Examination reveals 12mm separation of symphysis pubis on palpation, positive ASLR test, and tenderness over pubic symphysis. What is the MOST appropriate management?

options.A: Aggressive stretching to restore pelvic mobility
options.B: High-impact exercises to strengthen pelvic floor
options.C: Pelvic support belt, limited weight-bearing as tolerated, gentle core stabilization, ice/analgesia; physician referral for significant diastasis
options.D: Complete bed rest for 8 weeks

explanation: Option C is correct. Symphysis pubis diastasis >10mm postpartum causes significant pain and instability. Management: pelvic belt for mechanical support, pain management, protected weight-bearing, gentle stabilization exercises as tolerated, possible crutches. 12mm separation may require orthopedic consultation (>15mm sometimes requires surgical fixation). Option A would increase pain/instability. Option B is contraindicated with acute instability and pain. Option D is too restrictive but some activity limitation is appropriate initially.

questionText: A 23-year-old patient with incomplete C5 ASIA C spinal cord injury (7 months post-injury) demonstrates: 4/5 biceps, 3/5 wrist extensors, 0/5 triceps, 0/5 hand intrinsics bilaterally. The patient is independent in manual wheelchair propulsion on level surfaces. What is the HIGHEST level of independence expected for ADLs?

options.B: Modified independence in feeding, grooming with adaptive equipment; minimal assist for dressing upper body; moderate-to-maximal assist lower body
options.C: Complete independence in all ADLs
options.D: Independence in all self-care except bladder/bowel management

explanation: Option B is correct. C5 incomplete (ASIA C with 3/5 wrist extensors suggests some C6 function) typically achieves: modified independence feeding/grooming with universal cuff, adapted utensils (using tenodesis grasp); upper body dressing with minimal assist and adaptive equipment; lower body dressing requires moderate assist; bladder/bowel program requires assist. The presence of some wrist extension improves function compared to complete C5. Option A underestimates functional potential. Option C is unrealistic for this level. Option D overestimates; complete dressing independence unlikely.

questionText: A 72-year-old patient with severe COPD (GOLD Stage IV, FEV1 28% predicted) uses 4L oxygen continuously and is enrolled in pulmonary rehabilitation. During 6-minute walk test, the patient completes 180 meters (predicted ~400m) with SpO2 dropping from 92% to 84% despite oxygen. What does this 6MWT distance indicate about prognosis?

options.B: Mild functional limitation
options.C: Moderate functional limitation
options.D: Severe functional limitation with poor prognosis; may be candidate for lung transplant or lung volume reduction evaluation

explanation: Option D is correct. Six-minute walk distance <200m in COPD patients is associated with poor prognosis, significantly increased mortality risk, and severe functional limitation. This patient at 180m with Stage IV COPD represents end-stage disease. Besides pulmonary rehabilitation, patient should be evaluated for advanced therapies: lung transplant candidacy (if age-appropriate and meets criteria), lung volume reduction surgery (if emphysema distribution appropriate), or palliative care discussion. Options A-C significantly underestimate severity; 180m vs 400m predicted is severe limitation.

questionText: A 55-year-old patient with hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency, severe <1%) is referred for physical therapy for chronic hemophilic arthropathy of bilateral knees and ankles. The patient is on prophylactic Factor VIII replacement. What exercise principle is MOST important for this population?

options.A: High-impact activities to build bone density
options.B: Aggressive passive ROM to prevent contractures
options.C: Regular low-impact exercise and strengthening timed with factor replacement; avoid contact sports and activities with high bleeding risk
options.D: Complete activity restriction to prevent bleeding

explanation: Option C is correct. Hemophilia patients benefit from exercise to: strengthen muscles (protects joints), maintain ROM, improve proprioception, reduce bleeding frequency. Key principles: low-impact activities (swimming, cycling, walking), avoid contact/collision sports, strengthen periarticular muscles, time exercise when factor levels optimal (after prophylaxis), monitor for joint bleeding. Regular exercise reduces spontaneous bleeds. Option A creates bleeding risk. Option B risks joint bleeding with aggressive motion. Option D leads to deconditioning, muscle weakness (actually increases bleeding risk).

questionText: A 47-year-old patient with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis presents with swan neck deformity of the index and middle fingers bilaterally (PIP hyperextension, DIP flexion). Hand function is significantly limited. The patient asks about splinting. What type of splint is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Static extension splint holding PIP in full extension
options.B: Ring splint blocking PIP hyperextension while allowing flexion
options.C: Resting hand splint holding all joints in neutral
options.D: Dynamic flexion splint to stretch PIP into more flexion

explanation: Option B is correct. Swan neck deformity (PIP hyperextension, DIP flexion) requires ring splint (oval-8 or silver ring splint) that blocks PIP hyperextension while allowing functional PIP flexion. This restores mechanical advantage of lateral bands and improves grip function. Allows functional hand use throughout day. Option A prevents all PIP motion, limiting function. Option C is for nighttime positioning, doesn't address functional problem during activities. Option D worsens the deformity by encouraging more PIP extension.

questionText: A 9-month-old infant (corrected age 7 months, born at 32 weeks) demonstrates: rolls prone-to-supine only, absent protective reactions, persistent asymmetric tonic neck reflex, lower extremity hypertonia, and inability to sit independently. The infant has good social interaction and tracking. What intervention approach is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Neurodevelopmental treatment focusing on postural control, integration of primitive reflexes, functional mobility training; family education
options.B: Cognitive stimulation activities as primary focus
options.C: Strengthening exercises only
options.D: Observation only as motor delays are normal for corrected age

explanation: Option A is correct. This infant shows signs of cerebral palsy: persistent ATNR (should integrate by 6 months), absent protective reactions (should emerge 6-9 months), hypertonia, and significant motor delays. Early intervention is critical: NDT techniques for postural control and movement quality, working toward developmental milestones, family training for positioning/handling, possible equipment needs. Good social interaction suggests cognitive sparing. Option B ignores motor deficits. Option C doesn't address tone, reflexes, or motor planning. Option D misses critical intervention window.

questionText: A 64-year-old patient post-COVID-19 (discharged from hospital 4 weeks ago, no ICU stay) reports severe debilitating fatigue, post-exertional malaise lasting days after minimal activity, difficulty concentrating, and exercise intolerance. Pre-COVID, patient was active and healthy. Cardiac workup is normal. What is this presentation MOST consistent with?

options.B: Deconditioning only
options.C: Post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC)/Long COVID
options.D: Chronic heart failure

explanation: Option C is correct. Post-exertional malaise (symptom worsening after activity persisting days), severe fatigue, cognitive dysfunction ('brain fog'), and exercise intolerance lasting >4 weeks post-acute infection defines Long COVID/PASC. This is distinct from simple deconditioning. Management requires: pacing (energy conservation, activity within tolerance without triggering PEM), gradual reconditioning below symptom threshold, symptom tracking, multidisciplinary care. Pushing through symptoms worsens condition. Option A/B underestimate complexity. Option D ruled out by cardiac workup.

questionText: A 68-year-old patient with arterial insufficiency ulcer on lateral malleolus has ABI 0.55 on affected limb. Vascular surgery performed angioplasty; post-procedure ABI improved to 0.78. Wound bed shows 80% granulation. What is the MOST appropriate wound care modification post-revascularization?

options.B: Continue non-compression protective dressing; monitor healing; compression still generally contraindicated with ABI <0.8
options.C: Apply heat to increase circulation
options.D: Begin aggressive sharp debridement
