explanation: Option B is correct. Post-revascularization with ABI improved to 0.78 represents improvement but still marginal perfusion (ABI 0.8-0.9 = mild PAD; <0.8 = moderate). While some sources suggest modified compression (15-20 mmHg) may be considered with ABI 0.6-0.8 under close supervision, standard teaching remains that compression is generally contraindicated below 0.8. Conservative approach: protective dressing, position for drainage, gentle exercise, monitor for healing improvement. Option A risks compromising revascularization. Option C on arterial ulcers is controversial. Option D unnecessary with healthy granulation.

questionText: A 25-year-old patient presents with insidious onset of low back pain over 6 months, morning stiffness lasting >1 hour improving with exercise, limited spinal extension, positive FABER test bilaterally, and reduced chest expansion (3cm). ESR is elevated. HLA-B27 is positive. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Mechanical low back pain
options.C: Lumbar disc herniation
options.D: Piriformis syndrome

explanation: Option A is correct. Classic presentation of ankylosing spondylitis: young adult (typically <40), inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness >30 min, improves with activity), reduced spinal mobility and chest expansion, positive HLA-B27, elevated ESR, bilateral SI joint involvement (FABER test). This is inflammatory arthritis requiring medical management (NSAIDs, biologics, exercise). Option B doesn't explain inflammatory signs or restricted chest expansion. Option C would have radicular symptoms. Option D doesn't cause reduced chest expansion or inflammatory markers.

questionText: A 55-year-old patient with well-controlled epilepsy (last seizure 3 years ago on medication) asks about exercise recommendations and safety. The patient wants to swim and cycle. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: No swimming or cycling due to seizure risk
options.B: Swimming and cycling are safe with no restrictions
options.C: Swimming only with supervision/buddy system; cycling with helmet on safe trails; generally safe to exercise with well-controlled epilepsy
options.D: Only indoor stationary cycling is allowed

explanation: Option C is correct. Well-controlled epilepsy (>1 year seizure-free) can safely exercise with reasonable precautions: swim with supervision/buddy (never alone), cycling with helmet on safe paths (avoid traffic), avoid activities where sudden loss of consciousness creates extreme danger (rock climbing, scuba). Exercise is beneficial and doesn't typically trigger seizures. Most restrictions are lifted after prolonged seizure-free period on medication. Option A is unnecessarily restrictive. Option B ignores basic safety in water. Option D is overly limiting.

questionText: A 59-year-old patient with diastolic heart failure (HFpEF, EF 58%) reports dyspnea with activities despite preserved ejection fraction. Exercise stress test shows reduced peak VO2. What exercise type is MOST beneficial for this specific type of heart failure?

options.B: Aerobic exercise with interval training; evidence suggests improves diastolic function and exercise capacity
options.C: No exercise as heart function is impaired
options.D: High-intensity resistance training only

explanation: Option B is correct. HFpEF (diastolic dysfunction with preserved EF) benefits specifically from aerobic exercise, particularly interval training. Studies show improvements in: peak VO2, diastolic function, exercise tolerance, and quality of life. The impaired relaxation and filling respond to aerobic conditioning. Resistance training can be added but aerobic is primary. Option A alone doesn't address the cardiovascular limitation. Option C is outdated; exercise is beneficial in HFpEF. Option D alone misses the aerobic component needed for diastolic improvement.

questionText: A 42-year-old patient with Crohn's disease in remission on infliximab (biologic immunosuppressant) and mesalamine wants to start exercising after months of illness. Recent colonoscopy shows healed mucosa. Labs show hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (low), otherwise stable. What exercise recommendation is MOST appropriate?

options.B: Gradual aerobic and resistance exercise program; monitor for disease flare signs; infection precautions
options.C: High-intensity exercise immediately to regain fitness
options.D: Abdominal strengthening exercises only

explanation: Option B is correct. IBD patients in remission benefit from exercise: improves fitness, reduces inflammation, enhances quality of life, doesn't trigger flares. Considerations: gradual progression due to deconditioning, mild anemia (may affect exercise tolerance), infection precautions on immunosuppressant, monitor for flare symptoms (bleeding, pain, diarrhea). Exercise during remission is encouraged. Option A is too restrictive. Option C is too aggressive after prolonged illness and with anemia. Option D is too limited and doesn't address cardiovascular reconditioning.

questionText: A 33-year-old patient presents with 8-month history of bilateral heel pain, worse with first steps in morning and after sitting. Examination reveals pain with palpation at medial calcaneal tubercle bilaterally, limited ankle dorsiflexion (0 degrees with knee extended, 10 degrees with knee flexed), and Body Mass Index of 35. Radiographs show bilateral heel spurs. What interventions should be PRIORITIZED?

options.B: Aggressive deep tissue massage to plantar fascia
options.C: Surgical release of plantar fascia
options.D: Gastrocnemius-soleus stretching, plantar fascia-specific stretching, night splints, weight loss counseling, and supportive footwear

explanation: Option D is correct. Plantar fasciitis first-line treatment: posterior chain stretching (gastroc-soleus shown most effective), plantar fascia-specific stretches, night splints, weight management (obesity is risk factor), supportive shoes/orthotics, activity modification. This addresses biomechanical causes (tight posterior structures, excessive load). Heel spurs are incidental finding, not cause. Options A-C are more aggressive interventions reserved for failed conservative treatment (typically after 6-12 months). Conservative approach should be comprehensive and prolonged before injections/surgery.

questionText: A 70-year-old patient presents with progressive gait instability, frequent falls, urinary urgency/incontinence, and cognitive slowing over 6 months. MRI shows enlarged ventricles with periventricular hyperintensities. What is this triad of symptoms MOST consistent with?

options.B: Parkinson's disease
options.C: Normal pressure hydrocephalus
options.D: Vascular dementia

explanation: Option C is correct. Normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) presents with classic triad: gait disturbance (magnetic/shuffling gait, falls), urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline ('wet, wacky, wobbly'). MRI shows ventriculomegaly. This is potentially reversible with ventriculoperitoneal shunt. Physical therapy role: gait training, fall prevention, but medical referral essential for definitive treatment. Option A lacks gait/urinary prominence. Option B has tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia. Option D doesn't typically present with this specific triad and ventricular enlargement.

questionText: A 51-year-old patient with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) and ICD (implantable cardioverter-defibrillator) placement asks about return to exercise. The patient was previously very active in tennis and basketball. Genetic testing confirmed familial HCM. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Return to competitive tennis and basketball without restrictions
options.B: No exercise of any kind due to HCM and ICD
options.C: Low-to-moderate intensity recreational exercise avoiding competitive sports; individual risk stratification by cardiologist essential
options.D: Only upper extremity exercises to avoid ICD lead displacement

explanation: Option C is correct. HCM management is evolving, but current consensus: avoid high-intensity competitive sports and activities with high dynamic component (risk of sudden cardiac death, especially with family history). Low-to-moderate intensity recreational exercise generally considered safe after individual risk stratification (considering gradient, arrhythmia history, family history). ICD presence indicates high-risk features. Shared decision-making with cardiology required. Option A is unsafe given HCM. Option B is overly restrictive; moderate exercise is beneficial. Option D is unnecessarily limited.

questionText: A 45-year-old patient reports right shoulder pain for 3 weeks following painting a ceiling. Examination reveals pain with active shoulder abduction between 60-120 degrees (painful arc), positive empty can test, and full passive range of motion. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Subacromial impingement syndrome
options.C: Glenohumeral osteoarthritis
options.D: Biceps tendon rupture

explanation: Option B is correct. The painful arc sign (pain between 60-120 degrees of abduction) and positive empty can test (supraspinatus testing) are classic for subacromial impingement. Full passive ROM rules out adhesive capsulitis. The mechanism (overhead activity) and presentation fit impingement. Option A would show restricted passive ROM in capsular pattern. Option C would show limited ROM and crepitus. Option D would show visible deformity (Popeye sign) and weakness in elbow flexion.

questionText: A 72-year-old patient with right hemisphere stroke 4 weeks ago demonstrates left-sided neglect and impulsivity during transfers. The patient attempts to stand without locking the wheelchair brakes. What is the MOST appropriate intervention strategy?

options.A: Restrict the patient to bed rest for safety
options.B: Provide detailed verbal explanations before each transfer
options.C: Use visual cues on the left side and consistent step-by-step commands with supervision for safety
options.D: Allow independent transfers to promote autonomy

explanation: Option C is correct. Right hemisphere stroke causes left neglect and impaired safety awareness. Interventions should include: visual cues to draw attention to neglected side, consistent simple commands, environmental modifications, close supervision for safety. Teaching scanning strategies and using bright tape/markers on left side can improve awareness. Option A is too restrictive and prevents necessary rehabilitation. Option B is ineffective for patients with impaired safety judgment. Option D is unsafe given impulsivity and neglect.

questionText: A 66-year-old patient in Phase II cardiac rehabilitation completes 20 minutes of treadmill exercise. Pre-exercise HR was 72 bpm, peak exercise HR reached 118 bpm, and 5 minutes post-exercise HR is 112 bpm. What does the heart rate recovery indicate?

options.B: Abnormal heart rate recovery suggesting impaired autonomic function
options.C: Excessive cardiovascular fitness
options.D: No clinical significance

explanation: Option B is correct. Heart rate recovery (HRR) is the decrease in HR during the first minute (or 2 minutes) after exercise cessation. Normal HRR is ≥12 bpm decrease in first minute. This patient shows only 6 bpm decrease (118 to 112) over 5 minutes, which is abnormal. Impaired HRR is associated with increased cardiovascular mortality risk and suggests autonomic dysfunction. This should prompt closer monitoring and possible cardiology consultation. Options A and C are incorrect given the slow recovery. Option D underestimates clinical importance of HRR.

questionText: A 38-year-old patient presents with medial knee pain that worsens with descending stairs. Examination reveals tenderness along the medial joint line, positive McMurray's test, and no ligamentous laxity. What structure is MOST likely injured?

options.B: Medial collateral ligament
options.C: Anterior cruciate ligament
options.D: Patellar tendon

explanation: Option A is correct. Medial joint line tenderness with positive McMurray's test (clicking/pain with tibial rotation during flexion-extension) indicates meniscal pathology. Pain with descending stairs (compressive loading) is typical for meniscal tears. Absence of ligamentous laxity rules out MCL or ACL injury. Option B would show tenderness over the MCL itself and positive valgus stress test. Option C would demonstrate positive Lachman's or anterior drawer tests. Option D would show anterior knee pain at the patellar tendon insertion.

questionText: A 78-year-old patient with a Stage 2 pressure injury over the sacrum shows a partial-thickness wound with intact shallow crater. What is the MOST appropriate primary dressing?

options.A: Dry gauze dressing changed three times daily
options.B: Wet-to-dry dressing for debridement
options.C: Hydrocolloid or foam dressing to maintain moist wound environment
options.D: No dressing; leave open to air

explanation: Option C is correct. Stage 2 pressure injuries (partial-thickness with exposed dermis) require moist wound healing environment. Hydrocolloid or foam dressings maintain appropriate moisture, protect from friction/shear, and can be left in place for several days (reducing dressing change trauma). These promote faster healing than dry dressings. Option A dries the wound, impairing healing. Option B is not indicated for clean Stage 2 wounds without necrotic tissue. Option D exposes wound to contamination and drying.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with COPD demonstrates the following breathing pattern during evaluation: prolonged expiration, use of pursed-lip breathing, and forward-leaning posture. What is the PRIMARY reason for the forward-leaning posture?

options.B: To optimize diaphragm position and reduce work of breathing
options.C: To improve visual attention to tasks
options.D: To stretch the chest wall muscles

explanation: Option B is correct. Forward-leaning posture (tripod position) in COPD patients optimizes respiratory mechanics by: fixing the shoulder girdle to allow accessory muscles to assist breathing, reducing abdominal pressure on the diaphragm, and improving diaphragmatic length-tension relationship. This position naturally reduces work of breathing and improves ventilation. Pursed-lip breathing and forward lean are compensatory strategies COPD patients adopt for symptom relief. Option A may be incidental but isn't the primary reason. Options C and D are not related to the breathing mechanics.

questionText: A 5-year-old child with cerebral palsy demonstrates tight hip adductors causing scissoring gait pattern. Passive hip abduction is limited to 20 degrees bilaterally. What intervention should be included in the treatment plan?

options.A: Aggressive passive stretching into pain range
options.B: Strengthening of hip adductors
options.C: High-velocity thrust manipulation
options.D: Prolonged gentle stretching, positioning program, possible botulinum toxin consultation

explanation: Option D is correct. Spastic hip adductors in CP require multimodal management: prolonged gentle stretching (30-60 seconds holds), positioning program (abduction pillow, side-lying, standing frame), activity modification, and consideration of botulinum toxin injections when spasticity significantly limits function/ROM. Severe limitation (only 20 degrees) may benefit from toxin to allow more effective PT. Option A can trigger increased spasticity and is contraindicated. Option B would worsen scissoring. Option C is not appropriate for spasticity management.

questionText: A 50-year-old patient with type 2 diabetes reports numbness in both feet. Semmes-Weinstein monofilament testing shows inability to detect 5.07 (10g) monofilament on plantar surface. What is the MOST important patient education topic?

options.A: Aggressive foot massage to improve sensation
options.B: Walking barefoot to strengthen foot muscles
options.C: Daily foot inspection, proper footwear, and avoiding extreme temperatures
options.D: Applying heating pads for circulation

explanation: Option C is correct. Inability to detect 10g monofilament indicates loss of protective sensation, creating high risk for undetected injuries and ulceration. Critical education includes: daily visual foot inspection (using mirror for plantar surface), proper footwear always (never barefoot), checking inside shoes before wearing, avoiding extreme temperatures (burn/frostbite risk), immediate reporting of any injuries, and regular podiatry care. Option A doesn't restore sensation. Option B is dangerous with neuropathy. Option D risks burns with impaired sensation.

questionText: A 62-year-old patient presents to outpatient therapy 8 weeks post-myocardial infarction. The patient reports intermittent chest discomfort with yard work that resolves with rest. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.A: Immediately contact physician before proceeding with therapy
options.B: Begin gentle exercises and monitor symptoms
options.C: Provide education on energy conservation techniques
options.D: Proceed with standard cardiac rehabilitation protocol

explanation: Option A is correct. New or recurrent chest discomfort post-MI, even if brief and resolving with rest, suggests possible angina or ischemia and requires immediate physician evaluation before continuing therapy. This could indicate incomplete revascularization, restenosis, or new coronary event. Physical therapy should not proceed until medical clearance obtained and symptoms evaluated. Options B, C, and D all inappropriately continue therapy without medical clearance for symptoms suggesting cardiac ischemia.

questionText: A 32-year-old patient presents with anterior shoulder pain during throwing. Examination reveals pain and apprehension with shoulder in 90 degrees abduction and external rotation. Pain decreases when posterior pressure applied to humeral head. What test is being performed?

options.B: Hawkins-Kennedy test
options.C: Relocation test
options.D: Cross-body adduction test

explanation: Option C is correct. The relocation test positions the shoulder in the apprehension position (90° abduction, external rotation) which typically causes pain/apprehension in anterior instability. Applying posterior force to the humeral head relocates it in the glenoid, reducing anterior translation and relieving symptoms. This is specific for anterior glenohumeral instability. Option A (Neer) tests impingement via forced flexion. Option B (Hawkins-Kennedy) tests impingement via internal rotation at 90° flexion. Option D tests AC joint pathology.

questionText: A 68-year-old patient with Parkinson's disease demonstrates reduced step length and difficulty initiating gait. What intervention has the BEST evidence for improving gait parameters?

options.B: Visual cues (floor markers/lines) and auditory cues (metronome) during gait training
options.C: Treadmill training without cues
options.D: Static standing balance exercises only

explanation: Option B is correct. External cueing (visual lines/targets on floor, auditory rhythmic cues) is highly evidence-based for improving gait parameters in PD: increases step length, improves cadence, reduces freezing episodes. External cues bypass defective internal cueing mechanisms in the basal ganglia. Patients can be taught to use cues at home (transverse lines, metronome apps). Option A addresses only one component. Option C is beneficial but less effective than with cueing. Option D doesn't address gait-specific deficits.

questionText: A 65-year-old patient has a venous ulcer on the medial lower leg. Ankle-brachial index is 1.05. The wound has moderate drainage. What is the MOST appropriate intervention?

options.B: No compression due to drainage
options.C: Compression therapy at 50-60 mmHg
options.D: Multi-layer compression therapy 30-40 mmHg with absorbent dressing

explanation: Option D is correct. Venous ulcers with normal ABI (>0.9) require compression therapy as primary treatment (30-40 mmHg at ankle). Multi-layer compression provides sustained pressure, improves venous return, reduces edema and venous hypertension. Absorbent dressing handles drainage while compression addresses underlying pathology. Option A doesn't provide adequate venous return improvement. Option B is incorrect; drainage is not a compression contraindication with normal ABI. Option C exceeds recommended compression levels and risks discomfort/non-compliance.

questionText: A 55-year-old patient with COPD is learning breathing exercises. The patient currently breathes with upper chest and recruits accessory muscles at rest. Which breathing pattern should be taught FIRST?

options.B: Pursed-lip breathing
options.C: Segmental breathing
options.D: Paced breathing

explanation: Option A is correct. Diaphragmatic breathing should be taught first as the foundation for efficient breathing. It reduces accessory muscle use, decreases work of breathing, and improves ventilation. Once established, other techniques like pursed-lip breathing can be added. Teaching sequence: diaphragmatic breathing → pursed-lip breathing → combination during activities. Option B is important but builds on diaphragmatic control. Option C is more advanced. Option D is useful but secondary to establishing proper breathing mechanics.

questionText: A 28-year-old runner reports gradual onset of lateral hip pain over several weeks. Pain worsens with running, climbing stairs, and lying on the affected side. Examination reveals tenderness over greater trochanter and positive single-leg stance test. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (trochanteric bursitis)
options.C: Iliopsoas tendinitis
options.D: Femoral neck stress fracture

explanation: Option B is correct. Greater trochanteric pain syndrome presents with: lateral hip pain worse with activity and lying on side, tenderness over greater trochanter, positive single-leg stance (Trendelenburg), and often tight IT band. Common in runners. Treatment includes addressing hip abductor weakness, IT band stretching, and activity modification. Option A affects older adults with groin pain. Option C causes anterior hip/groin pain. Option D would show positive hop test and severe pain with weight-bearing.

questionText: A 3-year-old child demonstrates the following during evaluation: pulls to stand using furniture, cruises along furniture holding on, but does not walk independently. Motor development is otherwise age-appropriate. What should be the NEXT step?

options.A: Reassure family that development is normal
options.B: Refer immediately for neurological evaluation
options.C: Initiate physical therapy to work on independent ambulation; monitor closely as independent walking typically develops by 18 months
options.D: Recommend ankle-foot orthoses

explanation: Option C is correct. Independent walking typically develops by 15-18 months. At 3 years without independent ambulation (only cruising) represents significant delay requiring intervention. Physical therapy should address: balance, postural control, strength, and confidence for independent walking. Simultaneously, evaluate for underlying causes (muscle weakness, coordination issues, sensory concerns). Option A underestimates the delay. Option B may be needed if no progress, but PT should begin. Option D is premature without full evaluation.

questionText: A 48-year-old patient with asthma uses an albuterol inhaler. The patient asks when to use the inhaler relative to exercise. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Immediately after exercise to prevent symptoms
options.B: 15-30 minutes before exercise to prevent exercise-induced bronchospasm
options.C: During exercise when symptoms begin
options.D: Only if symptoms occur; not prophylactically

explanation: Option B is correct. Short-acting beta-agonists (albuterol) taken 15-30 minutes before exercise prevent exercise-induced bronchospasm (EIB) and allow for better exercise tolerance. This prophylactic use is recommended for patients with known EIB. Once bronchospasm begins, it's harder to reverse. Proper warm-up should also be emphasized. Option A is too late. Option C waits until symptoms develop. Option D doesn't prevent EIB when prophylactic use is appropriate.

questionText: A 55-year-old patient presents with neck pain radiating into the right arm following a motor vehicle accident 2 weeks ago. Examination reveals decreased sensation in the thumb and index finger, weak biceps (4-/5), and diminished biceps reflex. Which nerve root is MOST likely affected?

options.B: C6
options.C: C7
options.D: C8

explanation: Option B is correct. C6 nerve root compression presents with: lateral arm pain radiating to thumb/index finger, C6 dermatome sensory changes (lateral forearm, thumb, index finger), weak biceps and wrist extensors, diminished biceps reflex. This fits the clinical presentation. Option A (C5) affects deltoid, biceps strength, and lateral arm sensation but not thumb/index finger. Option C (C7) affects triceps, wrist flexors, middle finger. Option D (C8) affects hand intrinsics, ring/little finger.

questionText: A 72-year-old patient with diabetes has a plantar foot ulcer that has healed. The patient asks about preventing recurrence. What is the MOST important prevention strategy?

options.A: Apply moisturizing lotion between toes daily
options.B: Soak feet in hot water each evening
options.C: Walk barefoot at home to strengthen feet
options.D: Wear therapeutic shoes with custom insoles and perform daily foot inspections

explanation: Option D is correct. Preventing diabetic foot ulcer recurrence requires: therapeutic/extra-depth footwear with custom pressure-redistributing insoles, daily foot inspection (visual check with mirror for plantar surface), proper nail care, immediate attention to any problems, regular podiatry visits. 40% of diabetic foot ulcers recur within 1 year without proper prevention. Option A is good but lotion should not go between toes (moisture promotes fungal infection). Option B risks burns with neuropathy. Option C is dangerous; should always wear protective footwear.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with type 2 diabetes reports burning foot pain, worse at night, rated 6/10. Examination shows decreased sensation to light touch and 10g monofilament. Which intervention is MOST evidence-based for neuropathic pain?

options.B: Aggressive desensitization with rough textures
options.C: TENS unit and regular aerobic exercise
options.D: Complete avoidance of weight-bearing

explanation: Option C is correct. Evidence-based interventions for diabetic neuropathic pain include: TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation) for pain modulation, regular aerobic exercise (improves glycemic control and nerve function), and pharmacological management if prescribed. Exercise has direct benefits on neuropathy symptoms. Option A risks burns with impaired sensation. Option B is for hypersensitivity, not appropriate for loss of sensation with pain. Option D causes deconditioning without addressing pain.

questionText: A 64-year-old patient with chronic bronchitis has thick, tenacious secretions and reports difficulty clearing them. What airway clearance technique would be MOST beneficial?

options.B: Positive expiratory pressure (PEP) device followed by huff coughing
options.C: Incentive spirometry alone
options.D: Forceful coughing attempts

explanation: Option B is correct. PEP therapy helps mobilize secretions from peripheral airways by creating back pressure that keeps airways open during expiration. Following PEP with huff coughing (forced exhalation technique) effectively moves secretions proximally for expectoration. This combination is evidence-based for chronic bronchitis. Option A doesn't mobilize thick secretions. Option C focuses on inspiration, not secretion clearance. Option D can cause bronchospasm and is less effective than controlled huff coughing.

questionText: A 42-year-old patient presents with low back pain radiating into the right buttock and posterior thigh, stopping at the knee. Straight leg raise reproduces pain at 50 degrees. Neurological examination is normal. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Lumbar facet joint dysfunction or muscle strain
options.C: Cauda equina syndrome
options.D: Piriformis syndrome

explanation: Option B is correct. Referred pain to buttock and posterior thigh without neurological signs suggests somatic referred pain from facet joint, muscle, or ligamentous structures rather than nerve root compression. Pain stopping at knee (not foot) and normal neurological exam differentiate this from radiculopathy. Positive SLR can indicate hamstring tightness or non-specific back pain. Option A would show neurological deficits and pain to foot. Option C is medical emergency with bilateral symptoms and bowel/bladder changes. Option D causes deep buttock pain but positive FAIR test.

questionText: A 76-year-old patient demonstrates significant postural instability and has fallen twice in the past month. Berg Balance Scale score is 42/56. What does this score indicate?

options.A: Low fall risk; no intervention needed
options.B: Moderate fall risk; monitor only
options.C: High fall risk; intensive balance training and fall prevention strategies needed
options.D: Patient should be wheelchair-bound for safety

explanation: Option C is correct. Berg Balance Scale score 42/56 indicates high fall risk (scores <45 associated with increased fall risk; <40 indicates almost 100% fall risk). This requires: intensive balance training, environmental modifications, possible assistive device evaluation, fall prevention education, and possibly home safety evaluation. Interventions should address balance deficits while maintaining mobility. Option A significantly underestimates risk. Option B is insufficient. Option D is overly restrictive; mobility should be maintained with appropriate interventions.

questionText: A 52-year-old patient with history of smoking presents with productive cough for 3 months, wheezing, and dyspnea with exertion. Pulmonary function tests show FEV1/FVC ratio of 65% (normal >70%). What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Obstructive lung disease (COPD)
options.C: Normal pulmonary function
options.D: Pulmonary fibrosis

explanation: Option B is correct. FEV1/FVC ratio <70% defines obstructive lung disease. Combined with smoking history, chronic productive cough, and dyspnea, this indicates COPD (likely chronic bronchitis component). The ratio shows airflow limitation characteristic of obstruction. Option A would show reduced lung volumes with normal/high ratio. Option C is incorrect; ratio <70% is abnormal. Option D is a restrictive disease with reduced volumes but typically preserved ratio.

questionText: A 65-year-old patient 4 weeks post-total knee arthroplasty has achieved 95 degrees of knee flexion and full extension. The patient reports difficulty ascending stairs and standing from a chair. What exercise should be emphasized?

options.B: Passive ROM exercises
options.C: Quadriceps strengthening and functional exercises (sit-to-stand, step-ups)
options.D: Gait training only

explanation: Option C is correct. Difficulty with stairs and sit-to-stand indicates quadriceps weakness, the primary functional deficit post-TKA. At 4 weeks with adequate ROM (95° flexion is functional), emphasis should shift to strengthening and functional training. Progressive resistance exercises for quadriceps combined with task-specific training (sit-to-stand progressions, step-ups) address functional limitations. Option A alone doesn't address the primary weakness. Option B is not primary need with adequate ROM. Option D doesn't address strength deficit causing functional limitations.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient presents with a wound on the lateral malleolus. Ankle-brachial index is 0.68. What does this ABI indicate?

options.B: Mild peripheral arterial disease
options.C: Moderate peripheral arterial disease; compression therapy contraindicated
options.D: Venous insufficiency

explanation: Option C is correct. ABI 0.68 indicates moderate peripheral arterial disease (PAD): 0.9-1.3 is normal, 0.7-0.9 is mild PAD, 0.4-0.7 is moderate PAD, <0.4 is severe PAD/critical limb ischemia. Compression therapy is contraindicated with ABI <0.8 as it can further compromise arterial blood flow. This patient needs vascular evaluation for revascularization, not compression. Wound healing will be impaired without improved perfusion. Option A is incorrect. Option B underestimates severity. Option D measures venous disease, not ABI.

questionText: A 34-year-old patient with incomplete T12 spinal cord injury (ASIA C) is being evaluated for assistive device. The patient has 4/5 hip flexors and 3+/5 quadriceps bilaterally. What is the MOST appropriate mobility device?

options.B: Bilateral knee-ankle-foot orthoses (KAFOs) with walker or forearm crutches
options.C: No assistive device needed
options.D: Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) only

explanation: Option B is correct. T12 incomplete SCI with 3+/5 quadriceps has potential for ambulation with bilateral KAFOs for knee stability and walker or forearm crutches. This level typically achieves household to community ambulation with appropriate bracing and assistive devices. The good hip flexion and fair quadriceps allow for ambulation with support. Option A is too pessimistic given lower-level injury and fair quadriceps. Option C overestimates stability with 3+/5 quadriceps. Option D doesn't provide needed knee stability.

questionText: A 70-year-old patient in acute care post-pneumonia has the following arterial blood gas values: pH 7.32, PaCO2 50 mmHg, HCO3 25 mEq/L, PaO2 82 mmHg. What acid-base disorder is present?

options.B: Respiratory acidosis
options.C: Metabolic alkalosis
options.D: Respiratory alkalosis

explanation: Option B is correct. pH <7.35 indicates acidosis. PaCO2 >45 indicates respiratory component (hypoventilation/CO2 retention). HCO3 is normal (22-26), so no metabolic compensation yet, indicating acute respiratory acidosis. This can occur with pneumonia due to impaired ventilation. Treatment focuses on improving ventilation and treating underlying pneumonia. Option A would show low HCO3. Option C would show high pH. Option D would show low PaCO2.

questionText: A 48-year-old patient with osteoarthritis of the knee asks about exercise. The patient is concerned exercise will damage the joint further. What is the MOST evidence-based recommendation?

options.A: Avoid all exercise to protect the joint
options.B: High-impact activities only for bone density
options.C: Regular low-impact exercise including strengthening and aerobic activity; does not worsen OA and improves symptoms
options.D: Exercise only during periods without pain

explanation: Option C is correct. Strong evidence shows exercise does not worsen knee OA and significantly improves: pain, function, quality of life. Recommended activities include: low-impact aerobic (walking, cycling, swimming), quadriceps strengthening, and flexibility. Exercise is first-line treatment for knee OA. Option A leads to weakness and deconditioning, worsening functional limitations. Option B creates excessive joint stress. Option D prevents consistent beneficial effects; some discomfort during exercise is acceptable if it resolves quickly.

questionText: A 54-year-old patient reports gradually progressive weakness in both hands over 6 months, difficulty with buttons and gripping objects. Examination reveals muscle atrophy in the hands, fasciculations, and hyperreflexia. Sensation is intact. What condition do these findings suggest?

options.B: Peripheral neuropathy
options.C: Cervical radiculopathy
options.D: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)

explanation: Option D is correct. ALS presents with progressive weakness, muscle atrophy, fasciculations (lower motor neuron signs), and hyperreflexia (upper motor neuron signs) without sensory loss. The combination of UMN and LMN signs is pathognomonic for ALS. This requires immediate neurologist referral for diagnosis and management. Option A is localized to median nerve distribution, no UMN signs. Option B has sensory involvement, no UMN signs. Option C would have dermatomal pattern and might have sensory changes.

questionText: A 56-year-old patient with obesity (BMI 38) and sleep apnea uses CPAP nightly. The patient wants to start exercising for weight loss. What is the MOST appropriate initial exercise prescription?

options.A: High-intensity interval training to maximize calorie burn
options.B: Low-to-moderate intensity aerobic exercise (walking, cycling) starting 10-15 minutes and gradually progressing
options.C: Resistance training only
options.D: No exercise until significant weight loss achieved through diet

explanation: Option B is correct. Individuals with obesity and sleep apnea should start with low-to-moderate intensity, low-impact aerobic exercise, beginning with short duration (10-15 minutes) and gradually progressing frequency and duration before intensity. This approach improves adherence, reduces injury risk, and benefits both weight management and sleep apnea. Option A is too aggressive for deconditioned individual and may worsen OSA symptoms. Option C alone doesn't optimize calorie expenditure or cardiovascular benefits. Option D delays beneficial effects of exercise.

questionText: A 36-year-old patient presents with wrist pain on the thumb side, worse with gripping and lifting objects. Finkelstein test is positive. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Carpal tunnel syndrome
options.C: Scaphoid fracture
options.D: Thumb carpometacarpal arthritis

explanation: Option A is correct. De Quervain's tenosynovitis involves inflammation of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons in the first dorsal compartment. Positive Finkelstein test (pain with ulnar deviation while grasping thumb in palm) is diagnostic. Treatment includes splinting, activity modification, and strengthening after inflammation resolves. Option B involves median nerve with different symptoms. Option C would show anatomical snuffbox tenderness and trauma history. Option D affects thumb base joint with grinding/crepitus.

questionText: A 68-year-old patient has a Stage 3 pressure injury over the sacrum with 100% granulation tissue and decreasing size. The wound edges show epithelialization. What does this indicate about wound healing?

options.A: Wound is infected and requires antibiotics
options.B: Wound is healing appropriately; continue current treatment
options.C: Wound requires surgical debridement
options.D: Wound healing has stalled; change treatment completely

explanation: Option B is correct. Healthy granulation tissue (beefy red, not friable), decreasing wound size, and epithelialization at edges all indicate appropriate wound healing progression. Current treatment plan (appropriate dressing, pressure relief, nutrition) should be continued while monitoring progress. Weekly measurements track healing rate. Option A - healthy granulation doesn't indicate infection. Option C - no necrotic tissue requiring debridement described. Option D - wound shows clear signs of progress.

questionText: A 2-year-old child is being evaluated for delayed walking. The child can pull to stand and cruise along furniture but does not walk independently. The child has good cognitive and social skills. What is the MOST appropriate initial step?

options.A: Reassure parents that development is normal
options.B: Immediately refer for genetic testing
options.C: Initiate physical therapy evaluation and intervention for delayed motor milestones
options.D: Wait until age 3 to see if walking develops

explanation: Option C is correct. Independent walking typically develops by 15-18 months. At 2 years, only cruising represents significant motor delay requiring intervention. Physical therapy should evaluate strength, tone, balance, coordination, and provide interventions to facilitate independent ambulation. May need further medical evaluation if underlying cause suspected. Option A underestimates delay. Option B may be needed but PT should begin concurrently. Option D delays necessary early intervention during critical developmental period.

questionText: A 72-year-old patient with history of smoking presents with chronic cough and dyspnea. Pulmonary function tests show increased total lung capacity and residual volume, with decreased FEV1. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Asthma
options.C: Emphysema
options.D: Pulmonary fibrosis

explanation: Option C is correct. Emphysema (a type of COPD) causes: increased TLC and RV (hyperinflation due to air trapping), decreased FEV1 (obstruction), and loss of elastic recoil. The smoking history strongly supports emphysema. These PFT findings distinguish emphysema from other conditions. Option A would show decreased TLC and volumes. Option B typically shows reversible obstruction with normal TLC. Option D (restrictive) shows decreased TLC and volumes.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with rheumatoid arthritis reports morning stiffness lasting 45 minutes. Lab results show elevated ESR and CRP. What does prolonged morning stiffness indicate?

options.B: Active inflammatory disease
options.C: Overuse injury
options.D: Nutritional deficiency

explanation: Option B is correct. Morning stiffness >30 minutes (especially >1 hour) in RA indicates active inflammatory disease. Combined with elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), this suggests the disease is not well-controlled and may need medication adjustment. Physical therapy should modify intensity during active inflammation and coordinate with rheumatology. Options A and C don't explain inflammatory markers or the specific pattern. Option D is unlikely primary cause with elevated ESR/CRP and RA diagnosis.

questionText: A 66-year-old patient with type 2 diabetes reports bilateral foot numbness and occasional burning pain at night. Ankle reflexes are absent bilaterally. What is the MOST likely condition?

options.B: Peripheral arterial disease
options.C: Vitamin B12 deficiency
options.D: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy

explanation: Option D is correct. Diabetic peripheral neuropathy typically presents with bilateral stocking-glove distribution sensory loss, neuropathic pain (burning, tingling), and absent ankle reflexes. This is most common in long-standing diabetes. Management includes glycemic control, neuropathic pain management, fall prevention, and foot care education. Option A would have variable patterns and neurogenic claudication. Option B affects perfusion, not sensation primarily. Option C is possible but diabetes makes DPN more likely.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with congestive heart failure asks about fluid intake. The patient is on diuretics. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Drink as much water as possible to stay hydrated
options.B: Limit fluid intake to 2 liters (64 oz) per day and monitor daily weights
options.C: No fluid restrictions necessary
options.D: Restrict fluids to less than 1 liter per day

explanation: Option B is correct. Heart failure patients typically need fluid restriction (often 1.5-2 liters/day depending on severity) to prevent fluid overload and pulmonary congestion. Daily weight monitoring is crucial (report 2-3 pound gain in 24 hours or 5 pounds in one week). Fluid restriction combined with diuretics helps manage volume status. Option A can worsen heart failure. Option C ignores need for volume management. Option D may be too restrictive unless prescribed for severe HF.

questionText: A 44-year-old patient presents with clicking in the knee during squatting. McMurray's test is positive with lateral rotation. Where is the injury MOST likely located?

options.B: Lateral meniscus
options.C: Anterior cruciate ligament
options.D: Posterior cruciate ligament

explanation: Option A is correct. McMurray's test with lateral tibial rotation (external rotation) stresses the medial meniscus. Positive test (pain/click) indicates medial meniscal pathology. During the test, the tibia is rotated laterally while the knee is flexed and extended; if medial meniscus is torn, it gets trapped causing pain/click. Option B would be positive with medial tibial rotation. Options C and D would show instability tests positive (Lachman's, posterior drawer), not clicking with McMurray's.

questionText: A 75-year-old patient has a Stage 2 pressure injury on the heel. What intervention is MOST important for healing?

options.B: Massage around the wound margins
options.C: Complete pressure offloading of the heel
options.D: Apply ice to reduce inflammation

explanation: Option C is correct. Pressure relief is the most critical intervention for pressure injury healing. Heels are particularly challenging - complete offloading required (heel suspended off bed surface using pillow under calf, pressure-relief boots, or positioning). Without pressure removal, wound cannot heal regardless of dressing choice. Appropriate dressing maintains moist environment, but pressure relief is priority. Option A dries wound. Option B can damage fragile tissue. Option D is not indicated for pressure injuries.

questionText: A 7-year-old child with Duchenne muscular dystrophy is beginning to have difficulty climbing stairs. Gowers' sign is positive. What intervention is MOST appropriate at this stage?

options.B: Gentle stretching, positioning, and functional activities within tolerance; avoid overwork
options.C: Complete bed rest to preserve muscle function
options.D: Aggressive aerobic conditioning

explanation: Option B is correct. DMD management focuses on: maintaining function through gentle stretching (prevent contractures), positioning, functional activities within tolerance, and avoiding overwork weakness. Gowers' sign (using hands to push up from floor) indicates proximal weakness. Exercise must be carefully monitored to avoid damaging already-weak muscles. Option A can cause overwork damage in DMD. Option C leads to unnecessary functional loss and contractures. Option D can accelerate muscle damage.

questionText: A 64-year-old patient post-cardiac surgery develops atrial fibrillation. The patient is placed on warfarin (Coumadin). INR is 2.5. What is the MOST important precaution during physical therapy?

options.B: No special precautions needed
options.C: Avoid activities with high fall or trauma risk; monitor for signs of bleeding
options.D: Increase intensity to improve cardiovascular fitness

explanation: Option C is correct. Patients on anticoagulation with therapeutic INR (2-3 for atrial fibrillation) can exercise safely but require precautions: avoid contact sports or activities with high trauma/fall risk, monitor for bleeding signs (bruising, blood in urine/stool, unusual bleeding), proper footwear, safe environment. Fall prevention is critical. Non-contact aerobic and resistance exercises are generally safe. Option A is unnecessarily restrictive. Option B ignores bleeding risk with falls. Option D doesn't address safety concerns.

questionText: A 52-year-old patient presents with gradual onset low back pain, worse in morning and with prolonged sitting, improving with movement. Examination shows limited lumbar flexion and extension. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Lumbar spondylosis (degenerative disc disease)
options.C: Spondylolisthesis
options.D: Ankylosing spondylitis

explanation: Option B is correct. Lumbar spondylosis (degenerative changes) presents with: gradual onset mechanical back pain, morning stiffness that improves with movement, worse with sitting, limited ROM in all directions due to stiffness. This is age-appropriate degenerative change. Treatment focuses on movement, strengthening, and pain management. Option A typically has acute onset with radicular symptoms. Option C would show step-off palpably and specific symptoms. Option D is inflammatory with prolonged morning stiffness (>1 hour) and younger onset.

questionText: A 38-year-old patient reports headaches, dizziness, and blurred vision following a car accident 1 week ago. Cognitive testing shows impaired concentration and memory. What condition is MOST likely?

options.B: Migraine headache
options.C: Post-concussion syndrome
options.D: Cervical radiculopathy

explanation: Option C is correct. Post-concussion syndrome presents with: headaches, dizziness, cognitive difficulties (concentration, memory), vision changes, sensitivity to light/noise following head trauma. Management includes: gradual return to activity, vestibular rehabilitation if indicated, cognitive rest initially then gradual return to work/activities. Symptoms can persist weeks to months. Option A would have focal neurological deficits. Option B lacks the trauma history and cognitive symptoms. Option D causes radicular arm symptoms.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with COPD asks about the benefit of pulmonary rehabilitation. What is the PRIMARY benefit supported by research?

options.A: Cure COPD and restore normal lung function
options.B: Improve exercise tolerance, reduce dyspnea, and enhance quality of life
options.C: Prevent all future exacerbations
options.D: Eliminate need for supplemental oxygen

explanation: Option B is correct. Pulmonary rehabilitation has strong evidence for: improving exercise capacity and functional performance, reducing dyspnea perception, enhancing quality of life, reducing hospitalizations, and improving psychological well-being. These benefits occur despite no significant change in lung function (FEV1). PR includes exercise training, education, and self-management strategies. Option A - PR doesn't cure COPD or change PFTs significantly. Option C - reduces but doesn't prevent all exacerbations. Option D - oxygen needs depend on gas exchange, not PR.

questionText: A 60-year-old patient with osteoporosis (T-score -2.8) asks about safe exercises. Which activity is MOST appropriate?

options.B: High-impact aerobics class
options.C: Walking and light resistance training with weights
options.D: Spinal flexion exercises for core strength

explanation: Option C is correct. Weight-bearing exercise (walking) and progressive resistance training provide osteogenic stimulus to maintain/improve bone density while being safe for osteoporosis. Light-to-moderate weights with good form reduce fracture risk while providing benefits. Avoid excessive spinal flexion. Option A (swimming) is non-weight-bearing and doesn't provide adequate osteogenic stimulus. Option B may be too high-impact for T-score -2.8. Option D - excessive spinal flexion increases vertebral fracture risk in osteoporosis.

questionText: A 55-year-old patient with chronic venous insufficiency has healed venous ulcer. What long-term management is MOST important to prevent recurrence?

options.B: Elevate legs once weekly
options.C: Discontinue all compression since wound is healed
options.D: Wear compression stockings (20-30 mmHg) daily and elevate legs regularly

explanation: Option D is correct. Preventing venous ulcer recurrence requires lifelong management of venous insufficiency: compression stockings (20-30 mmHg) worn daily, regular leg elevation, exercise (calf muscle pump), skin care, weight management. Without ongoing compression, recurrence rate is 60-70% within 1 year. The underlying venous hypertension persists even after wound healing. Option A alone doesn't address venous hypertension. Option B is insufficient frequency. Option C leads to rapid recurrence.

questionText: A 70-year-old patient with Parkinson's disease demonstrates stooped posture, shuffling gait, and reduced arm swing. What intervention would MOST effectively address the postural changes?

options.A: Cervical collar to maintain upright posture
options.B: Postural awareness training, flexibility exercises for chest/hip flexors, and strengthening of extensors
options.C: Complete bed rest to prevent falls
options.D: Use of posterior walker only

explanation: Option B is correct. PD postural changes (flexed trunk, head forward) require: postural awareness training (mirrors, cueing), flexibility exercises for typically tight areas (pectorals, hip flexors), strengthening of extensors (back, hip extensors), and functional training. Education on maintenance strategies is important. Option A doesn't address the motor control issues and may cause dependence. Option C worsens deconditioning and postural changes. Option D alone doesn't retrain posture.

questionText: A 52-year-old patient with peripheral arterial disease reports calf pain after walking 2 blocks that resolves with 2-3 minutes of rest. What is this symptom pattern MOST consistent with?

options.B: Deep vein thrombosis
options.C: Chronic venous insufficiency
options.D: Peripheral neuropathy

explanation: Option A is correct. Intermittent claudication is the hallmark symptom of PAD: cramping/pain in leg muscles during exercise (due to inadequate arterial blood flow meeting increased metabolic demand), relieved predictably by rest. The consistent distance and quick relief with rest are classic. Treatment includes supervised exercise therapy and medical management. Option B causes constant pain and swelling. Option C causes aching that worsens with prolonged standing. Option D causes numbness/tingling, not exercise-induced cramping.

questionText: A 46-year-old patient presents with insidious onset of shoulder stiffness and pain over 4 months. Examination reveals limited passive and active ROM in all directions with capsular pattern (external rotation > abduction > internal rotation). What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Subacromial impingement
options.C: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)
options.D: Glenohumeral osteoarthritis

explanation: Option C is correct. Adhesive capsulitis presents with: gradual onset, significant loss of passive and active ROM (differentiates from rotator cuff issues), capsular pattern of restriction (external rotation most limited), and typically progresses through freezing, frozen, and thawing phases. Management includes gentle mobilization, stretching, and patience. Option A would have weakness but better passive ROM. Option B has full passive ROM. Option D is less common in this age group and typically has different history.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with multiple sclerosis reports increased fatigue and weakness after taking a hot shower. Symptoms improve after cooling down. What phenomenon is this?

options.B: Infection developing
options.C: Medication side effect
options.D: Uhthoff's phenomenon (heat sensitivity)

explanation: Option D is correct. Uhthoff's phenomenon describes temporary worsening of MS symptoms with increased body temperature (hot weather, hot shower, exercise, fever). Elevated temperature slows nerve conduction in demyelinated axons. Symptoms improve with cooling. Patient education important: cool environment for exercise, cooling strategies (fans, cool drinks, cooling vests), avoid hot showers/baths. This is NOT disease progression. Options A, B, and C don't explain the temperature-dependent pattern and reversibility.

questionText: A 68-year-old patient hospitalized with pneumonia has been on bed rest for 5 days. The patient wants to begin mobilizing. What is the MOST appropriate initial mobility activity?

options.B: Sitting at edge of bed with feet on floor, progressing to standing as tolerated
options.C: Active exercises in bed only for 3 more days
options.D: Transfer directly to chair for 1 hour

explanation: Option B is correct. After prolonged bed rest, progressive mobilization reduces orthostatic intolerance and deconditioning: start with head of bed elevation, progress to sitting at edge of bed (monitoring vitals, symptoms), then standing, then ambulation. This graduated approach allows cardiovascular adaptation and identifies tolerance. Option A is too aggressive after 5 days bedrest. Option C prolongs unnecessary immobility. Option D might be too much too soon without assessing tolerance to sitting.

questionText: A 42-year-old patient with hypothyroidism on levothyroxine reports fatigue and exercise intolerance. Recent TSH is 8.5 mIU/L (elevated, indicating undertreated hypothyroidism). What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.B: No exercise until energy improves
options.C: Communicate with physician about medication adjustment; gentle-to-moderate exercise as tolerated
options.D: Exercise has no benefit for hypothyroidism

explanation: Option C is correct. Elevated TSH indicates inadequate thyroid hormone replacement. The physician should be notified for medication adjustment. Meanwhile, gentle-to-moderate exercise as tolerated can help with energy and weight management, but patient may have reduced capacity until adequately treated. Once TSH normalizes, exercise tolerance typically improves. Option A may be too demanding with untreated hypothyroidism. Option B unnecessarily restricts activity. Option D is incorrect; exercise is beneficial once adequately treated.

questionText: A 29-year-old volleyball player presents with deep posterior shoulder pain during the acceleration phase of serving. Examination reveals positive O'Brien's test, positive anterior slide test, and deep pain with palpation of the posterior labrum. MRI confirms posterior-superior labral tear. What is the MOST appropriate initial management?

options.A: Immediate surgical referral for labral repair
options.B: 6-12 weeks of conservative treatment focusing on rotator cuff and scapular strengthening, followed by surgical consultation if symptoms persist
options.C: Complete shoulder immobilization for 6 weeks
options.D: Aggressive passive stretching to improve ROM

explanation: Option B is correct. Posterior-superior labral tears (including SLAP lesions) in athletes should initially be treated conservatively with comprehensive rehabilitation focusing on: rotator cuff strengthening, scapular stabilization, posterior capsule stretching, and gradual return to sport-specific activities. Many athletes (40-50%) improve with conservative treatment. Surgical referral is appropriate after 3 months of failed conservative care in appropriate candidates. Option A is premature; surgery is reserved for failed conservative treatment. Option C causes stiffness and muscle atrophy. Option D doesn't address the primary issue of dynamic instability and control.

questionText: A 5-year-old child presents with toe-walking, tight Achilles tendons bilaterally, and positive Babinski reflex. The child was born at 28 weeks gestation. Upper extremity function is age-appropriate. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Idiopathic toe-walking
options.C: Muscular dystrophy
options.D: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease

explanation: Option A is correct. This presentation is classic for spastic diplegic cerebral palsy: premature birth (major risk factor), lower extremity predominance with spasticity (tight heel cords, toe-walking), positive Babinski (upper motor neuron sign), and relatively spared upper extremities. Diplegia is the most common CP type in premature infants. Option B would not have positive Babinski or significant prematurity history. Option C shows progressive proximal weakness without spasticity. Option D is peripheral neuropathy with distal weakness, not spasticity.

questionText: A 61-year-old patient in ICU 48 hours post-esophagectomy develops acute respiratory distress. Vitals: HR 118 bpm, BP 88/52 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 88% on 4L O2. Breath sounds are diminished on the right with dullness to percussion. What complication is MOST likely?

options.B: Myocardial infarction
options.C: Pleural effusion or hemothorax
options.D: Pneumonia

explanation: Option C is correct. Post-esophagectomy, patients are at high risk for pleural effusion or hemothorax due to: surgical disruption of pleura, lymphatic drainage alterations, and fluid shifts. Unilateral diminished breath sounds with dullness to percussion strongly suggests fluid accumulation in pleural space. This requires immediate medical attention, possible chest tube placement. The acute presentation with hypotension and hypoxemia suggests significant accumulation. Option A typically presents with clear breath sounds and pleuritic pain. Option B doesn't explain unilateral findings. Option D would show crackles, not dullness.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with a venous ulcer has been wearing compression stockings (30-40 mmHg) daily for 6 months. The ulcer has healed completely. The patient asks if compression can be discontinued. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Discontinue compression immediately as wound is healed
options.B: Wear compression for 2 more weeks then stop
options.C: Reduce to 15-20 mmHg for 1 month then discontinue
options.D: Continue lifelong compression therapy (20-30 mmHg maintenance) to prevent recurrence

explanation: Option D is correct. Chronic venous insufficiency is a lifelong condition requiring ongoing management. After ulcer healing, patients should transition to maintenance compression stockings (typically 20-30 mmHg) worn daily, indefinitely. Without continued compression, recurrence rates are 60-70% within one year. The underlying venous hypertension persists even after wound closure. Education on proper stocking application, skin care, leg elevation, and exercise should continue. Options A, B, and C all lead to high probability of ulcer recurrence.

questionText: A 52-year-old patient with chronic low back pain demonstrates positive prone instability test and increased pain with extension-based activities. Imaging shows Grade I spondylolisthesis at L4-L5. What intervention should be the PRIMARY focus?

options.B: Williams flexion exercises only
options.C: Core stabilization exercises emphasizing transversus abdominis and multifidus
options.D: Aggressive spinal manipulation

explanation: Option C is correct. Positive prone instability test indicates segmental instability requiring spinal stabilization approach. Core stabilization focusing on deep stabilizers (transversus abdominis, multifidus, pelvic floor) provides dynamic stability to unstable segments. This has strongest evidence for spondylolisthesis management. Exercises progress from static holds to dynamic functional movements. Option A (extension) may worsen spondylolisthesis by increasing posterior element loading. Option B alone is too limited. Option D is relatively contraindicated with instability.

questionText: A 34-year-old patient presents 3 weeks postpartum with sudden onset of severe headache, right-sided weakness, and slurred speech. CT scan confirms left hemisphere hemorrhagic stroke. What is the MOST likely cause of stroke in this patient?

options.B: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis or eclampsia-related hemorrhage
options.C: Migraine headache
options.D: Multiple sclerosis

explanation: Option B is correct. Postpartum period (especially first 6 weeks) carries increased stroke risk due to: hypercoagulable state (cerebral venous sinus thrombosis), eclampsia/preeclampsia-related hemorrhage, cardioembolism, and arterial dissection. Hemorrhagic stroke in young woman postpartum suggests eclampsia-related hypertensive crisis or venous thrombosis. Immediate medical management critical. Rehabilitation may be complicated by postpartum factors. Option A is unlikely in 34-year-old without risk factors. Option C doesn't cause hemorrhagic stroke. Option D doesn't cause acute hemorrhagic stroke.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with chronic heart failure reports sleeping with 3 pillows at night due to difficulty breathing when lying flat. What is this symptom called?

options.B: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea
options.C: Dyspnea on exertion
options.D: Apnea

explanation: Option A is correct. Orthopnea is dyspnea that occurs when lying flat and is relieved by sitting up or elevating the head. In heart failure, lying flat increases venous return and pulmonary congestion. Patients often report number of pillows needed (e.g., 'three-pillow orthopnea'). This symptom suggests worsening heart failure and may need medical evaluation for medication adjustment. Option B is sudden breathlessness waking patient from sleep. Option C is breathlessness with activity. Option D is cessation of breathing.

questionText: A 48-year-old patient with systemic lupus erythematosus reports fatigue and joint pain. Lab results show low WBC (2,800/μL) and elevated ESR. The patient asks about exercise. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Avoid all exercise due to low WBC
options.B: High-intensity exercise to improve immune function
options.C: Moderate-intensity exercise with infection precautions; avoid exercise during acute flares
options.D: Exercise outdoors in sunlight for vitamin D

explanation: Option C is correct. SLE patients with mild leukopenia can exercise with precautions: moderate intensity, avoid exposure to infections (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts), monitor for flare symptoms, avoid UV exposure (photosensitivity). Exercise during remission periods improves cardiovascular health and reduces fatigue without worsening disease. During flares, reduce intensity or rest. Option A is too restrictive for mild leukopenia. Option B may trigger flares. Option D exposes to UV which triggers photosensitive rashes in SLE.

questionText: A 26-year-old basketball player sustained a high-energy knee injury with immediate swelling. Examination reveals positive Lachman's test (grade 3, no endpoint), positive anterior drawer, and 2+ effusion. Posterior drawer and valgus/varus stress tests are negative. What additional structure is MOST likely injured?

options.B: MCL only
options.C: LCL only
options.D: Medial or lateral meniscus

explanation: Option D is correct. ACL tears (indicated by positive Lachman's and anterior drawer with no endpoint) are commonly associated with meniscal injuries (40-50% concurrence). The high-energy mechanism and immediate swelling suggest significant internal derangement. Negative PCL and collateral ligament tests suggest isolated ACL, but meniscal injury is common accompaniment. MRI would confirm. This affects surgical planning and prognosis. Options A, B, and C would show positive stress tests for those structures.

questionText: A 68-year-old patient presents with gradual onset of hand weakness and muscle wasting over 8 months. Examination reveals fasciculations in both hands, weakness of hand intrinsics, and hyperreflexia in upper extremities. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.A: Immediate referral to neurologist for evaluation of possible motor neuron disease
options.B: Begin strengthening exercises for hand muscles
options.C: Splint hands in resting position
options.D: Reassure patient this is normal aging

explanation: Option A is correct. Combination of lower motor neuron signs (weakness, atrophy, fasciculations) and upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia) suggests motor neuron disease like ALS. This progressive, fatal condition requires immediate neurologist referral for diagnosis (EMG, clinical criteria) and multidisciplinary management. Early diagnosis allows for advance care planning, possible clinical trial enrollment, and symptomatic management. Options B, C, and D all delay critical diagnosis.

questionText: A 64-year-old patient with COPD uses pursed-lip breathing during activities. What is the PRIMARY physiological benefit of this technique?

options.B: Prevents premature airway collapse and improves gas exchange
options.C: Strengthens respiratory muscles
options.D: Decreases respiratory rate only

explanation: Option B is correct. Pursed-lip breathing creates back-pressure during exhalation that helps keep small airways open longer, preventing premature airway collapse (common in COPD due to loss of elastic recoil). This allows for more complete exhalation, reduces air trapping, improves gas exchange, and reduces work of breathing. It also naturally slows breathing rate. Patients often spontaneously adopt this technique. Option A is not the primary benefit. Option C occurs over time but isn't primary mechanism. Option D is a secondary benefit.

questionText: A 72-year-old patient with a chronic wound on the posterior heel shows the following: wound bed is 100% black eschar, no drainage, surrounding skin intact and dry, no odor, pedal pulses palpable. What is the MOST appropriate management?

options.A: Immediate aggressive sharp debridement of all eschar
options.B: Wet-to-dry dressings for mechanical debridement
options.C: Keep dry with dry dressing; assess vascular status and consider vascular consultation before debridement
options.D: Apply enzymatic debridement immediately

explanation: Option C is correct. Stable, dry eschar on heels with adequate perfusion (palpable pulses) may be left intact as it serves as biological dressing protecting underlying tissues. This is especially true for elderly patients. Before any debridement of heel eschar, vascular assessment is critical (ABIs, toe pressures). If perfusion adequate and no infection signs, dry eschar can remain. If inadequate perfusion, revascularization needed before debridement. Option A risks converting to larger wound or exposing bone if perfusion inadequate. Options B and D may compromise the stable dry environment.

questionText: A 42-year-old patient presents with insidious onset of lateral elbow pain over 2 months. Pain is worse with gripping and lifting coffee cup. Examination reveals tenderness 1 cm distal to lateral epicondyle and pain with resisted wrist extension. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Medial epicondylitis
options.C: Radial tunnel syndrome
options.D: Cubital tunnel syndrome

explanation: Option A is correct. Lateral epicondylalgia presents with: lateral elbow pain, tenderness at or just distal to lateral epicondyle, pain with resisted wrist extension (Cozen's test), and difficulty with gripping activities. Common in 40-50 year olds with repetitive wrist/hand use. Treatment includes activity modification, eccentric exercises, and counterforce bracing. Option B causes medial elbow pain. Option C causes more diffuse forearm pain and possible PIN symptoms. Option D causes medial elbow pain and ulnar nerve symptoms.

questionText: A 15-year-old with spina bifida at L2 level uses bilateral KAFOs and forearm crutches for household ambulation but wheelchair for community. The patient has developed a 35-degree thoracolumbar scoliosis. What is the MOST appropriate management approach?

options.A: Aggressive stretching program to correct curvature
options.B: Referral to orthopedic surgeon for evaluation; bracing unlikely to prevent progression; surgery may be indicated
options.C: No intervention as scoliosis doesn't affect function
options.D: Increase wheelchair use and discontinue ambulation

explanation: Option B is correct. Neuromuscular scoliosis in spina bifida typically progresses and bracing has limited effectiveness (unlike idiopathic scoliosis). At 35 degrees in adolescent with neuromuscular etiology, curve will likely progress. Surgical consultation indicated to discuss timing of spinal fusion. Goals include preventing progression that impairs sitting balance, pulmonary function, and quality of life. Curves >40-50 degrees or rapidly progressing typically require surgery. Option A cannot correct structural curve. Option C ignores functional implications. Option D unnecessarily limits mobility.

questionText: A 56-year-old patient 6 weeks post-myocardial infarction is performing treadmill exercise at 3.0 mph, 0% grade. Heart rate increases from 78 to 112 bpm, blood pressure from 118/76 to 142/80 mmHg, and RPE is 12/20. What do these responses indicate?

options.B: Excessive heart rate response
options.C: Inadequate blood pressure response
options.D: Signs of cardiac ischemia

explanation: Option A is correct. These are normal hemodynamic responses to moderate exercise: HR increase of 34 bpm for moderate-intensity walking is appropriate, systolic BP increase of 24 mmHg is expected (typically 7-10 mmHg per MET), and RPE of 12 ('somewhat hard') correlates with moderate intensity. No symptoms reported. This indicates safe exercise tolerance at this workload. Options B and C are incorrect; these are appropriate responses. Option D would show signs like chest pain, ST changes, or abnormal BP response (decrease or excessive increase).

questionText: A 52-year-old patient with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%) asks about exercise timing relative to meals. The patient takes metformin only. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Exercise only in fasted state to maximize weight loss
options.B: Exercise immediately after eating to prevent hypoglycemia
options.C: Exercise 1-2 hours after meals when blood glucose is elevated; monitor for hypoglycemia
options.D: Timing doesn't matter with oral medications

explanation: Option C is correct. For type 2 diabetes on oral medications: exercising 1-2 hours post-meal utilizes elevated blood glucose and improves glycemic control. Hypoglycemia risk is lower than with insulin but still possible. Monitoring blood glucose and carrying fast-acting carbs is prudent. Consistent timing helps establish routine. Option A creates excessive stress without food and hypoglycemia risk. Option B timing is too soon with active digestion. Option D ignores glucose management; timing does matter for optimal control.

questionText: A 38-year-old patient underwent anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) at C5-C6 four weeks ago. The patient complains of increased difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) over the past week. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.A: Immediately contact surgeon; worsening dysphagia post-ACDF may indicate complication
options.B: Reassure patient this is normal and will resolve
options.C: Provide swallowing exercises
options.D: Recommend soft diet and recheck in 2 weeks

explanation: Option A is correct. While mild dysphagia is common immediately post-ACDF (from retraction and edema), it typically improves over first 2 weeks. WORSENING dysphagia at 4 weeks is concerning for complications: hematoma formation, hardware displacement, infection, or excessive swelling. This requires immediate surgical evaluation and possible imaging. This could represent a serious complication requiring intervention. Options B, C, and D all dangerously delay evaluation of potentially serious postoperative complication.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with diabetic peripheral neuropathy demonstrates absent ankle reflexes bilaterally and decreased proprioception. Which exercise is MOST important for fall prevention?

options.B: Passive range of motion exercises
options.C: Aquatic therapy only
options.D: Progressive balance training incorporating visual and vestibular compensation strategies

explanation: Option D is correct. Peripheral neuropathy impairs somatosensory feedback, requiring compensation with visual and vestibular systems. Balance training should incorporate: exercises with eyes open and closed, varied surfaces, dual-task activities, and functional movements. Teaching compensatory strategies (increased visual attention, appropriate footwear, environmental awareness) is critical. Option A alone doesn't address balance deficits. Option B doesn't challenge balance system. Option C in water removes the challenge of maintaining balance against gravity.

questionText: A 54-year-old patient with newly diagnosed pulmonary hypertension (mean PAP 38 mmHg) asks about exercise. The patient desaturates to 86% during 6-minute walk test. What is the MOST appropriate exercise recommendation?

options.A: Complete exercise restriction due to pulmonary hypertension
options.B: Supervised low-to-moderate intensity exercise with supplemental oxygen and close monitoring
options.C: High-intensity interval training to improve cardiovascular fitness
options.D: Independent home exercise program without restrictions

explanation: Option B is correct. Pulmonary hypertension patients can benefit from supervised exercise with appropriate modifications: low-to-moderate intensity (avoid excessive dyspnea/Borg <5), supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥90%, frequent rest periods, close monitoring for signs of right heart strain (excessive fatigue, presyncope, chest pain). Evidence shows supervised exercise improves functional capacity and quality of life without adverse events. Option A is outdated. Option C creates excessive right ventricular strain. Option D lacks needed monitoring and safety.

questionText: A 68-year-old patient with lymphedema following breast cancer treatment asks about exercise precautions. What is the MOST evidence-based recommendation?

options.A: Avoid all upper extremity exercise to prevent lymphedema worsening
options.B: Only aquatic exercise is safe
options.C: Gradual progressive resistance and aerobic exercise with compression garment; monitor for increased swelling
options.D: High-intensity exercise without restrictions

explanation: Option C is correct. Current evidence shows gradual progressive exercise (resistance and aerobic) is safe and beneficial for lymphedema when performed with: compression garment use during exercise, gradual progression, monitoring for symptom changes, and appropriate education. Exercise improves function, strength, and quality of life without worsening lymphedema. The key is gradual progression and monitoring. Option A is outdated and leads to deconditioning. Option B is unnecessarily restrictive. Option D lacks appropriate precautions and monitoring.

questionText: A 32-year-old patient presents with gradual onset of anterior knee pain worse with squatting and descending stairs. Examination reveals positive patellar grind test and no effusion. What is the MOST appropriate initial intervention?

options.A: Complete rest from all weight-bearing activities
options.B: Quadriceps strengthening emphasizing VMO, hip strengthening, activity modification
options.C: Patellar taping only
options.D: Immediate MRI and surgical consultation

explanation: Option B is correct. Patellofemoral pain syndrome is treated conservatively with: quadriceps strengthening (especially VMO for patellar tracking), hip strengthening (address proximal control of dynamic valgus), activity modification (avoid excessive squatting, stairs, prolonged sitting), and possible patellar taping as adjunct. This has strong evidence for reducing pain and improving function. Option A causes deconditioning. Option C alone doesn't address strength deficits. Option D is premature; conservative treatment should be attempted first.

questionText: A 76-year-old patient with mild cognitive impairment (MMSE 24/30) is receiving physical therapy. The patient forgets instructions between sessions. What teaching strategy would be MOST effective?

options.A: Provide detailed written instructions with complex terminology
options.B: Give all instructions verbally without repetition
options.C: Focus on cognitive retraining exercises only
options.D: Use simple step-by-step instructions, repetition, and written/pictorial home program; involve caregiver

explanation: Option D is correct. Teaching strategies for cognitive impairment include: simple clear instructions (one step at a time), frequent repetition, written instructions with pictures/photos, consistent routine, and caregiver education/involvement. Focus on functional activities using procedural memory. Avoid complex explanations. Option A uses overly complex approach. Option B doesn't provide memory support. Option C doesn't address the primary functional needs and goals.
