questionText: Manual muscle testing grade of 3/5 indicates:

options.B: Muscle contracts but no joint movement
options.C: Muscle moves joint through full range against gravity
options.D: Muscle moves joint against gravity and moderate resistance

explanation: Option C is correct. MMT grading scale: 0/5 = no contraction, 1/5 = trace contraction without movement, 2/5 = full ROM with gravity eliminated (sidelying/supported), 3/5 = full ROM against gravity but no resistance, 4/5 = full ROM against gravity and moderate resistance, 5/5 = full ROM against gravity and full/normal resistance. Grade 3 is the minimum functional strength (can work against gravity). Plus/minus grades can be added for finer discrimination.

questionText: During normal gait, the quadriceps muscle is MOST active during:

options.B: Loading response
options.C: Pre-swing
options.D: Terminal swing

explanation: Option B is correct. Quadriceps are most active during loading response (0-10% of gait cycle, initial contact through foot flat) to eccentrically control knee flexion and prevent collapse while accepting body weight. Also active during terminal swing to decelerate leg and position foot for heel strike. Minimal activity during mid-stance. Understanding muscle activity throughout gait cycle helps identify and address gait deviations and weakness patterns.

questionText: Cyanosis is a bluish discoloration of skin indicating:

options.B: Inadequate oxygenation (hypoxemia)
options.C: Anemia
options.D: Normal circulation

explanation: Option B is correct. Cyanosis is bluish discoloration of skin/mucous membranes caused by increased deoxygenated hemoglobin (>5 g/dL). Central cyanosis (tongue, lips, oral mucosa) indicates serious hypoxemia from cardiac or pulmonary disease. Peripheral cyanosis (fingers, toes) may indicate poor circulation. Late sign of hypoxemia (SpO2 typically <85-90%). May be difficult to detect in dark skin; check mucous membranes. Cyanosis requires immediate medical attention.

questionText: Ataxia refers to:

options.B: Increased muscle tone
options.C: Decreased sensation
options.D: Lack of coordination and balance

explanation: Option D is correct. Ataxia is impaired coordination and balance, affecting gait, posture, and limb movements. Typically indicates cerebellar dysfunction or proprioceptive loss. Cerebellar ataxia shows intention tremor, dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia. Sensory ataxia shows positive Romberg test (worsens without vision). Results in wide-based, unsteady gait. Types include truncal ataxia (trunk/balance) and limb ataxia (extremity movements). Important to identify type and cause for appropriate treatment.

questionText: The normal lumbar spine lordosis angle is approximately:

options.B: 40-60 degrees
options.C: 70-80 degrees
options.D: 90-100 degrees

explanation: Option B is correct. Normal lumbar lordosis (measured using various methods) ranges approximately 40-60 degrees, though values vary by measurement technique and individual variation. Excessive lordosis (hyperlordosis, >60 degrees) may cause lower back pain. Decreased lordosis (<40 degrees, flat back) may also cause dysfunction. Thoracic kyphosis is normally 20-40 degrees. Cervical lordosis is normally 20-40 degrees. These curves provide shock absorption, balance, and distribute loads throughout spine.

questionText: Undermining in a wound is defined as:

options.A: Tissue destruction under intact skin at wound margins
options.B: Black necrotic tissue on wound surface
options.C: Pink granulation tissue formation
options.D: New epithelial cells at wound edges

explanation: Option A is correct. Undermining is tissue destruction beneath intact skin at wound edges, creating a pocket or cave-like appearance. Skin edge extends over destroyed tissue, creating dead space. Measured by gently inserting cotton-tipped applicator under wound edge and documenting extent using clock positions (12=head, 6=feet). Distinguished from tunneling (narrow tract extending away from wound). Both complications prolong healing and require specific treatment approaches including packing to eliminate dead space.

questionText: Jugular venous distension (JVD) is a sign of:

options.B: Hypotension
options.C: Right-sided heart failure or fluid overload
options.D: Left-sided heart failure only

explanation: Option C is correct. JVD (visible distension of external jugular veins when sitting at 45 degrees) indicates elevated central venous pressure from: right-sided heart failure, fluid overload, pulmonary hypertension, cardiac tamponade, or superior vena cava obstruction. Normal jugular veins collapse when upright at 45 degrees. JVD suggests volume overload or impaired right heart function. Often seen with peripheral edema and hepatomegaly. Important physical examination finding requiring medical evaluation.

questionText: The Timed Up and Go (TUG) test measures:

options.B: Cognitive function
options.C: Respiratory endurance
options.D: Functional mobility and fall risk

explanation: Option D is correct. TUG test measures functional mobility: patient rises from chair, walks 3 meters, turns around, walks back, sits down. Time is recorded. Interpretation: <10 seconds = normal, 10-20 seconds = good mobility/low fall risk, 20-30 seconds = problems with mobility/moderate fall risk, >30 seconds = severe mobility impairment/high fall risk. Quick, simple screening tool for balance, gait, functional mobility, and fall risk. Widely used in geriatric, neurological, and orthopedic populations.

questionText: Valgus stress test of the knee assesses the integrity of:

options.B: Medial collateral ligament
options.C: Anterior cruciate ligament
options.D: Posterior cruciate ligament

explanation: Option B is correct. Valgus stress test evaluates MCL integrity. Performed with knee at 0 degrees (tests MCL and capsule) and 30 degrees (isolates MCL). Therapist applies lateral force to ankle while stabilizing femur, stressing medial structures. Positive test shows excessive gapping or pain compared to opposite side. Grade 1 = <5mm opening (mild sprain), Grade 2 = 5-10mm (moderate), Grade 3 = >10mm with no endpoint (complete tear). Varus stress tests LCL (opposite direction).

questionText: Clubbing of the fingernails is associated with:

options.B: Diabetes mellitus
options.C: Chronic hypoxemia from cardiopulmonary disease
options.D: Hypertension

explanation: Option C is correct. Clubbing is bulbous enlargement of terminal phalanges with loss of normal angle between nail and nail bed (>180 degrees). Indicates chronic tissue hypoxia from: chronic lung disease (COPD, pulmonary fibrosis, cystic fibrosis), cyanotic heart disease, lung cancer, or inflammatory bowel disease. Develops over months to years of hypoxemia. Schamroth sign: loss of normal diamond-shaped window when opposing nail beds of same fingers. Indicates need for thorough cardiopulmonary evaluation.

questionText: Epithelialization in wound healing represents:

options.B: Presence of necrotic tissue
options.C: Wound infection
options.D: Formation of new skin cells migrating across wound bed

explanation: Option D is correct. Epithelialization is migration of epithelial cells from wound edges across granulation tissue bed, forming new skin surface. Appears as pink/white tissue advancing from margins toward center, or as islands within wound. Final phase of healing, closes wound surface. Requires moist environment and clean granulation tissue base. Supported by appropriate dressings (maintain moisture, don't damage fragile new tissue). Full epithelialization indicates wound closure, though tissue remains fragile and continues remodeling.

questionText: The Modified Ashworth Scale measures:

options.B: Muscle strength
options.C: Range of motion
options.D: Sensation

explanation: Option A is correct. Modified Ashworth Scale assesses muscle tone/spasticity severity: 0 = no increase in tone, 1 = slight increase with catch at end of ROM, 1+ = slight increase with catch followed by minimal resistance, 2 = marked increase but part moves easily, 3 = considerable increase in tone making passive movement difficult, 4 = affected part rigid. Performed by moving limb through passive ROM at moderate speed. Subjective measure but widely used clinically. Important for monitoring spasticity treatment effectiveness.

questionText: The normal Q-angle for females is:

options.B: 8-12 degrees
options.C: 15-18 degrees
options.D: 20-25 degrees

explanation: Option C is correct. Q-angle (quadriceps angle) is formed by line from ASIS to mid-patella and line from mid-patella to tibial tuberosity. Normal values: males 10-15 degrees, females 15-18 degrees (wider pelvis creates greater angle). Excessive Q-angle (>20 degrees) increases lateral patellar tracking force and is risk factor for patellofemoral pain syndrome, patellar subluxation/dislocation. Measured with patient supine, legs extended, quadriceps relaxed. Influences knee biomechanics and treatment planning.

questionText: Dyspnea is defined as:

options.B: Subjective sensation of difficult or labored breathing
options.C: Slow breathing
options.D: Absence of breathing

explanation: Option B is correct. Dyspnea is subjective sensation of shortness of breath or difficulty breathing, not necessarily correlated with respiratory rate or objective measurements. Described as 'breathlessness,' 'air hunger,' or 'inability to get enough air.' Assessed using scales like Borg dyspnea scale (0-10) or verbal descriptors (mild, moderate, severe). Distinguished from: tachypnea (rapid breathing, Option A), bradypnea (slow breathing, Option C), and apnea (absence of breathing, Option D). Important symptom in cardiopulmonary disease.

questionText: Proprioception refers to:

options.B: Sense of temperature
options.C: Sense of joint position and movement
options.D: Sense of touch

explanation: Option C is correct. Proprioception is sense of static joint position and movement (kinesthesia) without visual input. Receptors in muscles, tendons, joints, and ligaments provide feedback about limb position in space. Tested with eyes closed: therapist moves joint passively and patient identifies position or direction. Impaired proprioception causes ataxia, balance deficits, falls, and difficulty with fine motor control. Common with peripheral neuropathy, stroke, joint injury. Requires specific retraining exercises.

questionText: The 'unhappy triad' (terrible triad) of knee injuries involves damage to:

options.B: ACL, PCL, medial meniscus
options.C: LCL, PCL, lateral meniscus
options.D: ACL, MCL, medial meniscus

explanation: Option D is correct. The unhappy triad (O'Donoghue's triad) classically involves ACL tear, MCL tear, and medial meniscus tear, typically from valgus force with external rotation (common in contact sports). Modern understanding recognizes lateral meniscus may be injured as frequently as medial. This combination creates significant instability requiring comprehensive rehabilitation or surgical intervention. Mechanism: foot planted, lateral blow to knee causing valgus stress and external tibial rotation, overwhelming multiple structures simultaneously.

questionText: The term 'hemoptysis' means:

options.B: Difficulty swallowing
options.C: Chest pain
options.D: Rapid heart rate

explanation: Option A is correct. Hemoptysis is coughing up blood or blood-tinged sputum from respiratory tract. May indicate serious conditions: tuberculosis, lung cancer, pulmonary embolism, bronchitis, pneumonia, or trauma. Amount ranges from blood-streaked sputum to massive hemorrhage (>100-600 mL/24hrs). Distinguished from hematemesis (vomiting blood - GI source). Always requires medical evaluation to determine source and cause. Option B is dysphagia. Option C is angina or chest pain. Option D is tachycardia.

questionText: Moisture-associated skin damage (MASD) is caused by:

options.B: Inadequate nutrition
options.C: Prolonged exposure to moisture (urine, feces, wound drainage, perspiration)
options.D: Friction only

explanation: Option C is correct. MASD results from prolonged skin contact with moisture sources: incontinence (urine/feces), wound exudate, perspiration, or other body fluids. Causes skin maceration, erythema, breakdown. Types include: incontinence-associated dermatitis (IAD), intertriginous dermatitis, periwound moisture damage. Prevention includes: frequent cleansing, moisture barriers, absorbent products, management of incontinence/wound drainage. Distinguished from pressure injuries (though may coexist). Requires different treatment approach focusing on moisture management.

questionText: A patient with left hemiplegia following stroke would be expected to have weakness on the:

options.B: Right side of the body
options.C: Both sides equally
options.D: Lower extremities only

explanation: Option A is correct. Hemiplegia means paralysis of one side of body. Left hemiplegia indicates weakness/paralysis of left side, resulting from RIGHT hemisphere stroke (motor pathways cross at medullary pyramids). Right hemisphere stroke also typically causes: left-sided neglect, impulsivity, poor safety awareness, visuospatial deficits. Right hemiplegia results from LEFT hemisphere stroke, often with aphasia, apraxia, and cautious behavior. Understanding lesion location predicts impairments and guides treatment.

questionText: The term 'kyphosis' refers to:

options.B: Anterior curvature of the spine
options.C: Posterior curvature of the spine
options.D: Absence of spinal curves

explanation: Option C is correct. Kyphosis is posterior (backward) curvature of spine, normal in thoracic region (20-40 degrees). Excessive thoracic kyphosis (>40-45 degrees, 'hunchback' or 'dowager's hump') may result from: osteoporotic compression fractures, Scheuermann's disease, poor posture, or ankylosing spondylitis. Increases risk of balance problems, restrictive lung disease, and pain. Option A describes scoliosis. Option B describes lordosis. Option D describes flat spine (abnormal loss of normal curves).

questionText: Percussion note that sounds hollow or drum-like indicates:

options.B: Air-filled space (pneumothorax or emphysema)
options.C: Fluid-filled space (pleural effusion)
options.D: Consolidated lung tissue (pneumonia)

explanation: Option B is correct. Percussion produces different sounds based on underlying tissue density. Hyperresonance (hollow, drum-like, very loud) indicates excessive air: pneumothorax, emphysema. Normal lungs produce resonance (low-pitched, hollow). Dullness (thud-like, quiet) indicates solid/fluid-filled tissue: pleural effusion (Option C), pneumonia consolidation (Option D), tumor, or atelectasis. Flatness (very dull) over dense structures like liver or bone. Percussion is valuable bedside assessment technique.

questionText: The Functional Independence Measure (FIM) scores activities from:
explanation: Option B is correct. FIM assesses 18 activities of daily living in 6 domains (self-care, sphincter control, transfers, locomotion, communication, social cognition). Each item scored 1-7: 1 = total assistance, 2 = maximal assistance, 3 = moderate assistance, 4 = minimal assistance, 5 = supervision/setup, 6 = modified independence (device), 7 = complete independence. Total score 18-126. Widely used in rehabilitation settings to document function, track progress, and predict discharge needs. Higher scores indicate greater independence.

questionText: The primary function of the rotator cuff muscles is to:

options.B: Elevate the scapula
options.C: Flex the elbow
options.D: Stabilize the glenohumeral joint during movement

explanation: Option D is correct. Rotator cuff (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis) primarily provides dynamic stability to glenohumeral joint by maintaining humeral head position in glenoid fossa during arm movements. They compress and depress humeral head, counteracting superior migration forces from deltoid. While they assist with rotation and abduction (supraspinatus initiates abduction), stabilization is primary function. Deltoid provides power for elevation. This understanding guides rehabilitation focusing on stability before strength.

questionText: Autolytic debridement works by:

options.A: Using the body's own enzymes and moisture to break down necrotic tissue
options.B: Applying sharp instruments to cut away dead tissue
options.C: Using larvae (maggots) to consume dead tissue
options.D: Applying wet-to-dry gauze dressings

explanation: Option A is correct. Autolytic debridement uses body's own enzymes (proteases), moisture, and white blood cells to break down necrotic tissue. Achieved by maintaining moist wound environment with hydrocolloid, hydrogel, or transparent film dressings. Selective (only removes necrotic tissue), painless, and doesn't damage healthy tissue. Slower than other methods but appropriate for uninfected wounds. Option B describes sharp/surgical debridement. Option C describes biological debridement. Option D is mechanical debridement (non-selective).

questionText: The target heart rate zone for moderate-intensity aerobic exercise is:

options.B: 50-70% of maximum heart rate
options.C: 70-85% of maximum heart rate
options.D: 85-95% of maximum heart rate

explanation: Option B is correct. Exercise intensity guidelines: light intensity = 40-50% HRmax, moderate intensity = 50-70% HRmax, vigorous intensity = 70-85% HRmax, very hard intensity = 85-95% HRmax. For health benefits, adults should accumulate 150 minutes/week moderate or 75 minutes/week vigorous exercise. Maximum heart rate estimated as 220-age (though has limitations). Alternatively, use heart rate reserve (Karvonen formula) or RPE scale for intensity monitoring.

questionText: Apraxia is:

options.B: Muscle weakness
options.C: Inability to perform learned purposeful movements despite having physical ability
options.D: Lack of coordination

explanation: Option C is correct. Apraxia is inability to perform previously learned skilled movements despite having adequate strength, coordination, sensation, and comprehension. Types include: ideomotor (can't perform on command but can spontaneously), ideational (can't sequence multi-step tasks), constructional (can't copy or construct designs). Results from cortical lesions (stroke, dementia). Distinguished from weakness, ataxia, or comprehension deficits. Requires specific treatment approaches focusing on task practice and cueing.

questionText: The normal range of motion for knee flexion is:

options.B: 0-120 degrees
options.C: 0-135 degrees
options.D: 0-160 degrees

explanation: Option C is correct. Normal knee flexion ROM is 0-135 degrees (some sources state 130-140 degrees due to individual variation). Functional ROM for activities: walking requires ~70 degrees, stairs ~90 degrees, sitting in chair ~90-100 degrees, tying shoes ~110-120 degrees, squatting ~120-135 degrees. Limited flexion impacts function; functional goals should target activities patient needs to perform. Extension is 0 degrees (neutral, fully straight); hyperextension 5-10 degrees may be normal in some individuals.

questionText: The pressure generated by the heart during ventricular contraction is measured as:

options.B: Diastolic blood pressure
options.C: Mean arterial pressure
options.D: Pulse pressure

explanation: Option A is correct. Systolic blood pressure is peak pressure in arteries during ventricular contraction (systole), when heart pumps blood into circulation. Diastolic BP (Option B) is pressure during ventricular relaxation (diastole) when heart fills. Mean arterial pressure (Option C) is average pressure throughout cardiac cycle [MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP-DBP)]. Pulse pressure (Option D) is difference between systolic and diastolic (SBP-DBP). Understanding BP components guides interpretation of cardiovascular status.

questionText: A wound with a red, beefy appearance and moderate drainage is MOST likely in which phase of healing?

options.B: Proliferative phase
options.C: Remodeling/maturation phase
options.D: Infection phase

explanation: Option B is correct. Red, beefy, granular appearance with moderate drainage indicates proliferative phase (granulation tissue formation, typically days 4-21). This phase involves: angiogenesis (new blood vessels creating red color), fibroblast proliferation (collagen deposition), and epithelialization beginning at edges. Option A (days 0-4) shows inflammation, edema, minimal drainage. Option C (day 21-2 years) shows contraction, scar maturation, reduced vascularity (paler). 'Infection phase' isn't a normal healing phase; infection shows purulent drainage, odor.

questionText: Dysarthria is:

options.B: Inability to find words
options.C: Inability to read
options.D: Slurred or unclear speech due to muscle weakness

explanation: Option D is correct. Dysarthria is motor speech disorder resulting from weakness, paralysis, or incoordination of speech muscles (lips, tongue, vocal cords, diaphragm). Speech is slurred, slow, quiet, or has abnormal rhythm/pitch. Language and comprehension are intact (distinguishes from aphasia). Common in stroke, brain injury, Parkinson's, ALS, cerebral palsy. Option A describes receptive aphasia. Option B describes expressive aphasia/anomia. Option C is alexia. Each requires different treatment approach.

questionText: The FABER test (Patrick's test) is used to assess:

options.B: Ankle stability
options.C: Hip pathology and sacroiliac joint dysfunction
options.D: Lumbar disc herniation

explanation: Option C is correct. FABER (Flexion, ABduction, External Rotation) test: patient supine, test leg in figure-4 position (foot on opposite knee), examiner applies downward pressure on test knee and opposite ASIS. Positive test (pain or limited motion) may indicate: hip joint pathology (arthritis, labral tear, FAI), iliopsoas tightness, or SI joint dysfunction. Location of pain helps differentiate: anterior groin = hip, posterior = SI joint. Also called Patrick's test. Useful screening for hip/pelvis pathology.

questionText: Diaphoresis refers to:

options.B: Difficulty breathing
options.C: Chest pain
options.D: Irregular heart rhythm

explanation: Option A is correct. Diaphoresis is profuse sweating beyond what's needed for thermoregulation. May indicate: myocardial infarction, heart failure, hypoglycemia, shock, anxiety, medication side effect, or infection. When associated with chest pain, suggests cardiac event requiring immediate attention. Cold, clammy diaphoresis particularly concerning for MI or shock. Option B is dyspnea. Option C is angina. Option D is arrhythmia. Diaphoresis is important clinical sign requiring investigation of underlying cause.

questionText: The Brunnstrom stages of motor recovery after stroke range from:

options.B: Stages 1-5
options.C: Stages 1-7
options.D: Levels 1-10

explanation: Option C is correct. Brunnstrom stages describe motor recovery pattern after stroke: Stage 1 = flaccidity, no voluntary movement; Stage 2 = spasticity develops, minimal voluntary movement; Stage 3 = voluntary synergy patterns; Stage 4 = movement deviating from synergy; Stage 5 = increasing independence from synergy; Stage 6 = isolated joint movement, coordination improving; Stage 7 = normal motor function. Not all patients progress through all stages. Guides treatment selection based on current stage and potential for advancement.

questionText: Which wound requires the MOST concern for infection risk?

options.B: Abrasion with intact surrounding skin
options.C: Superficial first-degree burn
options.D: Deep puncture wound with contamination

explanation: Option D is correct. Puncture wounds have high infection risk due to: deep penetration creating anaerobic environment (ideal for tetanus, other bacteria), difficult to clean thoroughly, small opening may seal trapping bacteria, often contaminated by penetrating object. Require thorough irrigation, debridement if needed, tetanus prophylaxis, close monitoring. Options A-C have lower infection risk with proper care. Clean surgical wounds (Option A) have lowest infection risk (<2%). Understanding wound characteristics guides infection prevention and monitoring.

questionText: The scapulohumeral rhythm describes the relationship between:

options.B: Scapular and humeral movement during shoulder elevation
options.C: Clavicle and sternum movement
options.D: Scapula and ribs movement

explanation: Option B is correct. Scapulohumeral rhythm is coordinated movement of scapula and humerus during arm elevation. Normal ratio is 2:1 (glenohumeral:scapulothoracic). First 30 degrees primarily glenohumeral, then both contribute: for 180 degrees total elevation, ~120 degrees from glenohumeral joint, ~60 degrees from scapular upward rotation. Altered rhythm (often more scapular, less GH motion) indicates: rotator cuff pathology, capsular restriction, pain, or scapular dyskinesis. Assessment guides treatment focus.

questionText: Normal adult tidal volume at rest is approximately:

options.B: 500 mL (or 5-7 mL/kg)

explanation: Option B is correct. Tidal volume (TV) is volume of air moved in or out during normal breathing at rest, approximately 500 mL or 5-7 mL/kg ideal body weight. With respiratory rate ~12-20 breaths/min, minute ventilation = TV × RR = ~6-8 L/min. TV increases with exercise up to ~50-60% of vital capacity. Reduced TV with restrictive lung disease. Mechanical ventilation often set at 6-8 mL/kg for lung protective strategy. Understanding lung volumes aids in assessing respiratory function.

questionText: The 10-Meter Walk Test measures:

options.B: Static balance
options.C: Upper extremity coordination
options.D: Cognitive function

explanation: Option A is correct. 10-Meter Walk Test assesses gait speed: patient walks 10 meters at preferred or fast pace, time is recorded for middle 6 meters (allows acceleration/deceleration). Speed calculated in meters/second. Interpretation: <0.4 m/s = household ambulator, 0.4-0.8 m/s = limited community ambulator, >0.8 m/s = community ambulator. Predicts falls, functional mobility, community ambulation ability. Quick, simple, reliable measure used across many conditions. Can be performed with/without assistive device.

questionText: A 55-year-old male presents with limited right shoulder abduction. Evaluation of the scapulohumeral rhythm reveals that the scapula begins to upwardly rotate immediately upon initiation of abduction. Which of the following muscles is MOST likely weak or inhibited, leading to this early scapular 'shrug'?

options.B: Upper Trapezius
options.C: Serratus Anterior
options.D: Teres Major

explanation: Normal scapulohumeral rhythm involves a 'setting phase' where the scapula is relatively stable during the first 30° to 60° of abduction. (A) is correct because the supraspinatus is responsible for initiating abduction and depressing the humeral head. If it is weak, the deltoid acts as an unopposed upward puller, causing the humeral head to abut the acromion, forcing the patient to compensate with early scapular elevation (shrugging) to clear the subacromial space.

questionText: A patient is recovering from a large myocardial infarction and is being monitored in the ICU. The therapist notes a sudden change on the ECG: the P-waves and QRS complexes are completely independent of each other, with a ventricular rate of 35 bpm. What is the MOST likely diagnosis and the corresponding clinical priority?

options.A: First-degree AV block; continue with bedside range of motion.
options.B: Second-degree AV block (Mobitz II); monitor blood pressure during sitting.
options.C: Atrial Fibrillation; notify the nurse and check for a pulse.
options.D: Third-degree (Complete) AV block; initiate emergency protocols as this is a medical emergency.

explanation: In Third-degree AV block, there is no conduction between the atria and ventricles (AV dissociation), leading to independent P-waves and a slow ventricular 'escape' rhythm. (D) is correct because this rhythm significantly compromises cardiac output and can lead to cardiac arrest; it requires immediate medical intervention (often a pacemaker).

questionText: A patient with a Left Middle Cerebral Artery (MCA) stroke presents with Broca's Aphasia. Which of the following communication strategies is MOST appropriate for the physical therapist to use during gait training?

options.A: Ask the patient to explain the steps of the gait cycle back to you.
options.B: Use short, simple sentences and allow the patient ample time to respond with one-word answers or gestures.
options.C: Speak loudly and slowly to account for the patient's likely hearing loss.
options.D: Use only written instructions, as reading comprehension is typically fully preserved in Broca's aphasia.

explanation: Broca's aphasia (non-fluent aphasia) is characterized by difficulty with speech production, while comprehension is relatively preserved. (B) is correct because it minimizes the burden of production for the patient. (A) is too complex for a non-fluent patient. (C) is incorrect as aphasia is a language processing disorder, not a hearing impairment. (D) is incorrect as reading and writing are often also impaired to varying degrees.

questionText: During a manual muscle test for the Lower Trapezius, the patient is prone with the arm in 145° of abduction and the thumb pointing up. As the therapist applies downward pressure on the distal humerus, the patient's scapula is observed to tilt anteriorly and the medial border lifts off the rib cage. Which muscle is MOST likely substituting for the weak Lower Trapezius?

options.B: Rhomboids
options.C: Pectoralis Minor
options.D: Levator Scapulae

explanation: (C) is correct because the Pectoralis Minor attaches to the coracoid process; when the posterior stabilizers (Lower Trapezius) are weak, the Pectoralis Minor can pull the scapula into anterior tilt and protraction as it attempts to assist in stabilizing the shoulder girdle during the test.

questionText: A patient with Cystic Fibrosis is performing postural drainage and percussion. The therapist is targeting the Posterior Basal segments of both Lower Lobes. What is the correct position for this patient?

options.A: Prone with the head down (Trendelenburg position) and pillows under the hips.
options.B: Supine with the head down (Trendelenburg position) and knees flexed.
options.C: Left sidelying with the head of the bed elevated 30°.
options.D: Prone with the bed flat and pillows under the abdomen.

explanation: To drain the posterior basal segments, gravity must assist the flow from the posterior-inferior portion of the lungs toward the main bronchi. (A) is correct; the prone Trendelenburg position (head down approx. 18-20 inches) is the standard for these segments.

questionText: A 40-year-old female presents with fluctuating double vision (diplopia) and ptosis that worsens toward the end of the day. She also reports difficulty chewing tough meats. Physical examination shows normal deep tendon reflexes and no sensory loss. Which condition is MOST consistent with this presentation?

options.B: Myasthenia Gravis
options.C: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome
options.D: Guillain-Barré Syndrome

explanation: Myasthenia Gravis (MG) is an autoimmune disorder affecting the acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction. (B) is correct because its hallmark is fatiguability (worsening with use, better with rest) specifically affecting cranial muscles (eyes, chewing, swallowing). (A) would typically show UMN signs. (C) usually improves with repeated muscle use. (D) is an acute, ascending paralysis with absent reflexes.

questionText: A 22-year-old soccer player sustains a non-contact knee injury involving sudden deceleration and a change of direction. On examination, the therapist performs the 'Pivot Shift' test. What exactly is this test assessing, and what constitutes a positive result?

options.A: PCL integrity; the tibia sags posteriorly when the knee is flexed to 90°.
options.B: MCL integrity; the joint line opens under valgus stress at 20° of flexion.
options.C: ACL integrity/Anterolateral instability; the tibia subluxes anteriorly in extension and reduces posteriorly around 20-30° of flexion.
options.D: Meniscal tear; a 'clunk' is felt during knee extension with internal rotation.

explanation: The Pivot Shift is a highly specific test for ACL deficiency. (C) is correct. It assesses anterolateral rotatory instability. In an ACL-deficient knee, the tibia is subluxed anteriorly when the knee is in extension; as the knee is moved into flexion with a valgus stress and internal rotation, the iliotibial band pulls the tibia back into a reduced position, creating a 'shift' or 'clunk'.

questionText: A physical therapist is evaluating a patient's peripheral vascular system. The patient reports cramp-like pain in the calves that consistently occurs after walking 2 blocks and is relieved by 5 minutes of rest. The patient's Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) is 0.65. What is the MOST appropriate classification of this patient's condition?

options.A: Normal vascular status; the pain is likely musculoskeletal.
options.B: Moderate Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) with intermittent claudication.
options.C: Severe PAD with risk of critical limb ischemia.
options.D: Venous insufficiency; the patient should wear compression stockings.

explanation: An ABI between 0.5 and 0.8 is generally classified as moderate PAD. (B) is correct because the symptoms (intermittent claudication) and the ABI value of 0.65 align with moderate arterial obstruction. (A) is incorrect as ABI < 0.9 is abnormal. (C) is typically an ABI < 0.5. (D) is incorrect as claudication is an arterial, not venous, symptom.

questionText: A patient with a T4 complete Spinal Cord Injury (ASIA A) is performing a wheelie in their wheelchair to navigate a curb. They suddenly report a pounding headache and appear flushed. Their blood pressure is 180/100 mmHg. What is the first and most critical action the therapist should take?

options.A: Bring the patient to an upright seated position and check for a kink in the urinary catheter.
options.B: Lay the patient supine immediately and elevate their legs to improve venous return.
options.C: Administer an emergency dose of nitroglycerin and call 911.
options.D: Instruct the patient to perform a Valsalva maneuver to lower their heart rate.

explanation: The symptoms describe Autonomic Dysreflexia, a medical emergency common in SCI patients above T6. (A) is correct because the priority is to lower blood pressure by keeping the patient upright (orthostatic effect) and then identifying and removing the noxious stimulus (usually a full bladder or kinked catheter). (B) is contraindicated as laying them down will further increase intracranial pressure.

questionText: A 12-year-old male athlete reports insidious onset of groin and thigh pain and demonstrates a protective limp. During the physical exam, the therapist notes that when the patient's hip is passively flexed, the hip spontaneously moves into external rotation. Which condition is MOST likely?

options.B: Osgood-Schlatter Disease
options.C: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis (SCFE)
options.D: Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH)

explanation: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis (SCFE) typically occurs in adolescent males (10-16 years). (C) is correct because the 'Drehmann Sign' (obligatory external rotation during hip flexion) is a classic clinical indicator of SCFE due to the displacement of the femoral head. (A) usually occurs in younger children (4-8 years).

questionText: A patient is undergoing an exercise stress test. At a workload of 6 METs, the ECG shows 2mm of downsloping ST-segment depression. The patient is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate interpretation and action?

options.A: This is a normal response to exercise; continue the test to maximum effort.
options.B: This indicates myocardial ischemia; the test should be terminated immediately.
options.C: This is a sign of pericarditis; continue exercise but monitor chest pain.
options.D: This represents a bundle branch block; slow the treadmill speed but do not stop.

explanation: Horizontal or downsloping ST-segment depression ≥ 2mm is a strong indicator of myocardial ischemia. (B) is correct because even in an asymptomatic patient, this degree of depression is a relative/absolute indication to stop an exercise test depending on the specific protocol (ACSM guidelines). (A) is dangerous. (C) and (D) are incorrect interpretations of the ECG finding.

questionText: A therapist is treating a patient with Multiple Sclerosis (MS) who reports significant fatigue and heat sensitivity (Uthoff's phenomenon). Which of the following exercise parameters is MOST appropriate to prevent an exacerbation of symptoms?

options.A: High-intensity aerobic training in a warm therapeutic pool (94°F).
options.B: Exercising to the point of 'muscle failure' to maximize strength gains.
options.C: Low-to-moderate intensity exercise with frequent rest breaks in a climate-controlled room (68°F).
options.D: Perform exercise sessions only in the late evening when the patient's core temperature is highest.

explanation: Heat sensitivity is a hallmark of MS. (C) is correct because keeping the patient cool and using a distributed practice schedule (frequent rests) prevents the temporary worsening of neurological symptoms associated with increased core temperature. (A), (B), and (D) all increase core temperature or fatigue excessively.

questionText: A patient presents with persistent 'clicking' and pain in the temporomandibular joint (TMJ) during opening. The therapist notes that the click occurs early in the opening phase and is accompanied by a 'reciprocal' click during closing. What is the MOST likely clinical diagnosis?

options.B: Disc displacement without reduction.
options.C: Capsular fibrosis.
options.D: TMJ Subluxation.

explanation: (A) is correct. Reciprocal clicking (one during opening, one during closing) is the definitive sign of disc displacement with reduction. The disc starts displaced anteriorly, 'reduces' back onto the condyle during opening (first click), and then slips off again during closing (second click). (B) would involve a lack of clicking and limited opening ('closed lock').

questionText: A physical therapist is reading a patient's chart and notes a Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF) of 30%. What is the MOST likely clinical implication for this patient's exercise program?

options.A: The patient has excellent cardiac reserve and can perform high-intensity sports.
options.B: The patient has significant heart failure and will likely demonstrate reduced exercise tolerance and a blunted BP response.
options.C: The patient is at high risk for a pulmonary embolism during aerobic exercise.
options.D: The patient has a restrictive lung disease and will require supplemental oxygen at all times.

explanation: A normal LVEF is 55-70%. (B) is correct because an EF of 30% indicates severe systolic dysfunction (Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction). This patient will have limited stroke volume and cardiac output, necessitating a very gradual and monitored exercise progression. (A) is incorrect. (C) and (D) are unrelated to LVEF.

questionText: A patient with a complete T10 Spinal Cord Injury (SCI) wants to use Bilateral Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthoses (KAFOs) for functional community ambulation. Which of the following is the MOST realistic assessment of this goal?

options.A: This is a realistic goal; T10 level has full hip flexor control.
options.B: This is unrealistic; community ambulation requires at least L4 level innervation.
options.C: The patient can likely achieve 'exercise' or 'household' ambulation with KAFOs, but community ambulation is highly taxing and unlikely to be functional.
options.D: The patient will only be able to ambulate using a robotic exoskeleton, not KAFOs.

explanation: Functional community ambulation typically requires intact hip flexors (L2) and some knee extension (L3). (C) is correct because T10 patients lack hip control; while they can use a 'swing-through' or 'swing-to' gait with KAFOs for exercise, the energy expenditure is so high that they usually rely on a wheelchair for community mobility. (A) is incorrect (T10 does not have hip flexors).

questionText: A 48-year-old female presents with a 'frozen shoulder' (Adhesive Capsulitis) in the freezing stage. Which of the following joint mobilization techniques is MOST indicated to improve her significantly limited external rotation?

options.A: Grade III posterior glides of the humerus.
options.B: Grade II inferior glides of the humerus.
options.C: Grade III anterior glides of the humerus.
options.D: Grade IV medial glides of the humerus.

explanation: According to the convex-concave rule, external rotation of the humerus (convex) on the glenoid (concave) involves a posterior roll and an anterior glide. (C) is correct; anterior glides specifically address the restriction in external rotation. (A) would improve internal rotation. (B) would improve abduction.

questionText: An 82-year-old patient with pneumonia has a pulse oximetry reading (SpO2) of 88% while breathing room air. The patient is also slightly confused. What is the MOST appropriate next step for the physical therapist?

options.A: Begin airway clearance techniques immediately to clear secretions.
options.B: Withhold physical therapy, apply supplemental oxygen if ordered, and notify the nursing/medical staff.
options.C: Encourage the patient to perform deep breathing and re-check the SpO2 in 10 minutes.
options.D: Proceed with walking the patient to improve lung expansion.

explanation: An SpO2 of < 90% in an acute setting (pneumonia) indicates hypoxemia, and confusion is a sign of central nervous system hypoxia. (B) is correct; clinical stability must be established before proceeding with activity. (D) is dangerous and could lead to further desaturation.

questionText: A patient is evaluated 2 days after a traumatic brain injury and demonstrates decerebrate posturing. Which of the following best describes this posture and its clinical significance?

options.A: Full extension of the upper and lower extremities; indicates a lesion in the brainstem (midbrain to pons).
options.B: Flexion of the upper extremities and extension of the lower extremities; indicates a lesion above the red nucleus.
options.C: Flexion of all four limbs; indicates a cortical lesion.
options.D: Flaccid paralysis of all four limbs; indicates a spinal shock phase.

explanation: Decerebrate posturing (extension of all 4 limbs) (A) is generally more severe than decorticate (flexion of UEs, extension of LEs) and suggests a lesion lower in the brainstem. (B) describes decorticate posturing.

questionText: A 35-year-old weightlifter presents with pain on the radial side of the wrist that is aggravated by thumb movements. The Finkelstein test is positive. What structure is primarily involved in this condition?

options.B: Extensor Pollicis Longus
options.C: Adductor Pollicis
options.D: Abductor Pollicis Longus and Extensor Pollicis Brevis

explanation: De Quervain’s Tenosynovitis affects the first dorsal compartment of the wrist. (D) is correct; this compartment contains the APL and EPB. The Finkelstein test stretches these tendons over the radial styloid to reproduce pain.

questionText: During a Phase I cardiac rehab session, a patient's heart rate increases from 70 bpm (rest) to 110 bpm during a short walk in the hallway. The patient has a history of a recent MI. Is this a normal or abnormal response?

options.A: Normal; the heart rate should double with any physical activity.
options.B: Abnormal; in Phase I post-MI, the heart rate should not increase more than 20 bpm above resting.
options.C: Normal; an increase of 40 bpm is expected during the first week of recovery.
options.D: Abnormal; the heart rate should remain within 5 bpm of the resting rate.

explanation: Standard safety guidelines for Phase I cardiac rehab (post-MI) state that the HR should not exceed resting HR + 20 bpm (or resting HR + 30 bpm for post-surgical patients). (B) is correct. An increase from 70 to 110 (40 bpm increase) is too high for this early stage.

questionText: A patient with Cerebellar Ataxia demonstrates significant trunk instability and a wide-based gait. Which of the following coordination tests would be MOST specific for identifying dysmetria?

options.B: Finger-to-Nose test.
options.C: Romberg test.
options.D: Heel-to-Shin test while standing.

explanation: Dysmetria is the inability to judge distance or range of movement. (B) is correct; the finger-to-nose test (or finger-to-finger) is the classic test for terminal tremor and dysmetria. (A) tests for dysdiadochokinesia. (C) tests for sensory ataxia (proprioception).

questionText: A patient presents with a 'positive' Trendelenburg sign on the right side. During gait, which of the following compensations is the therapist MOST likely to observe?

options.A: The right side of the pelvis drops during the right swing phase.
options.B: The left side of the pelvis drops during the left swing phase.
options.C: The patient leans their trunk to the left during the left stance phase.
options.D: The patient leans their trunk to the right during the right stance phase.

explanation: A right Trendelenburg sign means the right hip abductors (Gluteus Medius) are weak. (D) is correct because a 'compensated' Trendelenburg involves leaning the trunk over the weak side (the right) to bring the center of mass over the hip joint and prevent the opposite (left) pelvis from dropping. (B) describes the 'uncompensated' pelvic drop.

questionText: A patient's ECG shows a PR interval that consistently measures 0.24 seconds, but every QRS complex is preceded by a P-wave. How should this rhythm be documented?

options.B: Second-degree AV block (Type I).
options.C: Second-degree AV block (Type II).
options.D: Normal Sinus Rhythm.

explanation: A normal PR interval is 0.12 to 0.20 seconds. (A) is correct; if the PR interval is prolonged (>0.20s) but every atrial impulse is conducted to the ventricles (no dropped beats), it is a First-degree AV block.

questionText: A patient with Brown-Séquard Syndrome at the T10 level (R-sided hemisection) would demonstrate which of the following clinical patterns?

options.A: Loss of pain and temperature on the right side; loss of motor function on the left side.
options.B: Loss of motor function and proprioception on the left side; loss of pain and temperature on the right side.
options.C: Loss of motor function and proprioception on the right side; loss of pain and temperature on the left side.
options.D: Total loss of all sensation and motor function below T10.

explanation: (C) is correct. Brown-Séquard results in ipsilateral (same side) loss of motor function and dorsal column sensations (proprioception/vibration) and contralateral (opposite side) loss of spinothalamic sensations (pain/temperature) starting a few segments below the lesion.

questionText: A patient with suspected Tibialis Posterior dysfunction exhibits a 'too many toes' sign. Which foot deformity is associated with this clinical finding?

options.B: Pes Planus with forefoot abduction.
options.C: Hallux Rigidus.
options.D: Equinovarus deformity.

explanation: The 'too many toes' sign is seen when viewing the patient from behind; the forefoot abducts, making the lateral toes visible. (B) is correct; this is characteristic of adult-acquired flatfoot due to posterior tibialis tendon insufficiency.

questionText: A physical therapist is evaluating a patient with Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD). The therapist notes that the patient has a 'barrel chest.' This physical finding is most directly caused by which physiological change?

options.B: Increased elastic recoil of the lung tissue.
options.C: Air trapping and pulmonary hyperinflation.
options.D: Excessive accumulation of pleural fluid.

explanation: (C) is correct. In COPD (especially emphysema), the loss of elastic recoil and airway collapse during exhalation leads to air trapping. This increases the Residual Volume and Functional Residual Capacity, forcing the rib cage into a chronically expanded, 'barrel' shape.

questionText: During an evaluation of a patient with a suspected vestibular disorder, the therapist performs the Head Thrust Test (Head Impulse Test). A positive result (corrective saccade when head is turned to the right) indicates a deficit in which structure?

options.B: Right Horizontal Semicircular Canal.
options.C: Central Vestibular Nuclei in the brainstem.
options.D: The Cerebellum.

explanation: The Head Thrust Test is a test of the Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex (VOR). (B) is correct because the test identifies the side of the peripheral vestibular lesion; if a corrective saccade occurs when the head is thrust to the *right*, it indicates the right-sided VOR is failing to keep the eyes on the target.

questionText: A patient with chronic Achilles tendinopathy is beginning a loading program. Based on current evidence, which type of exercise is MOST effective for reducing pain and improving function in this population?

options.B: Low-load isometric exercises held for 5 seconds.
options.C: Passive stretching of the gastrocnemius three times daily.
options.D: Complete rest and immobilization in a walking boot for 4 weeks.

explanation: (A) is correct. Eccentric loading (such as the Alfredson protocol) has the strongest evidence for treating mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy by promoting tendon remodeling. (D) is incorrect as total rest is detrimental to tendon health.

questionText: A therapist is performing chest PT on a patient with Bronchiectasis. The patient begins to cough up bright red blood (hemoptysis). What is the priority action?

options.A: Increase the intensity of percussion to clear the blood.
options.B: Encourage the patient to perform a forceful huff cough.
options.C: Switch to an incentive spirometer.
options.D: Stop all treatment, sit the patient up, and contact the physician immediately.

explanation: Active hemoptysis (D) is a contraindication to continued chest PT/postural drainage. It may indicate a significant airway bleed. (B) could worsen the bleeding.

questionText: A patient with a history of chronic alcoholism presents with gait ataxia, confusion, and nystagmus. Which nutritional deficiency is MOST likely responsible for these neurological symptoms?

options.B: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine).
options.C: Vitamin D.
options.D: Iron.

explanation: The triad of ataxia, confusion, and ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus) is characteristic of Wernicke’s Encephalopathy, caused by Thiamine (B1) deficiency (B), which is common in chronic alcoholism.

questionText: A 45-year-old male reports a 4-month history of dull, aching pain in the right groin and buttock that is aggravated by prolonged standing and walking. Physical examination reveals a significant loss of internal rotation (only 5°) and flexion (90°) of the right hip. There is a palpable 'hard' end-feel in all restricted ranges. Based on the Clinical Prediction Rule for hip osteoarthritis, which finding most strongly supports this diagnosis?

options.A: Age greater than 40 years and pain with hip extension.
options.B: Internal rotation less than 15° and hip flexion less than or equal to 115°.
options.C: Positive Scour test and pain with resisted hip abduction.
options.D: Morning stiffness that lasts for more than 2 hours.

explanation: According to the Altmann criteria/Clinical Prediction Rule for hip OA, the combination of hip internal rotation < 15°, hip flexion ≤ 115°, and age > 50 (though 45 is close) is highly indicative. (B) is the specific cluster that matches the research for identifying OA. (D) is incorrect because morning stiffness in OA typically resolves within 60 minutes; longer durations suggest Rheumatoid Arthritis.

questionText: A patient with a Left Middle Cerebral Artery (MCA) stroke demonstrates 'Ideomotor Apraxia.' When asked to demonstrate how to use a toothbrush, the patient is unable to perform the movement on command. Which of the following is the MOST accurate characteristic of this patient's condition?

options.A: The patient does not understand the concept or purpose of a toothbrush.
options.B: The patient can describe the steps of brushing teeth but lacks the motor strength to perform it.
options.C: The patient may spontaneously use the toothbrush correctly when at the sink in the morning, but fails when asked to 'mimic' the action.
options.D: The patient uses their finger as the toothbrush (body-part-as-object) and cannot correct the error.

explanation: Ideomotor apraxia is a breakdown between concept and execution. (C) is correct because these patients often perform the task automatically in the appropriate context but fail when asked to 'act it out' on command. (A) describes Ideational apraxia. (D) is a common error in apraxia but is not the defining neurophysiological characteristic compared to the automatic-voluntary dissociation.

questionText: A 62-year-old patient with Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) is participating in a pulmonary rehabilitation session. The patient's baseline SpO2 is 91%. During exercise, the SpO2 drops to 85%. According to Medicare guidelines and clinical best practices, what is the MOST appropriate action regarding supplemental oxygen?

options.A: Initiate or increase supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥ 90% during exertion.
options.B: Continue the exercise as long as the patient remains asymptomatic.
options.C: Stop the exercise and perform pursed-lip breathing until SpO2 returns to 91%, then resume without oxygen.
options.D: Titrate oxygen only if the patient's heart rate exceeds 120 bpm.

explanation: Medicare and clinical guidelines generally require supplemental oxygen for patients whose SpO2 drops to ≤ 88%. (A) is correct; for a patient with COPD, maintaining a saturation level ≥ 90% is the goal to prevent pulmonary hypertension and right-sided heart failure (cor pulmonale) caused by chronic hypoxic vasoconstriction.

questionText: A 35-year-old female patient with Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus is starting an aerobic exercise program. Her pre-exercise blood glucose is 85 mg/dL. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation for this patient prior to beginning her 30-minute walk?

options.A: Begin the exercise immediately, as exercise will naturally raise blood glucose.
options.B: Inject a bolus of short-acting insulin to prevent hyperglycemia during exercise.
options.C: Do not exercise today; any value below 100 mg/dL is a contraindication.
options.D: Consume a 15-30g carbohydrate snack and re-check blood glucose in 15-20 minutes.

explanation: (D) is correct. ACSM and ADA guidelines state that for patients with Type 1 DM, if pre-exercise glucose is < 100 mg/dL, a carbohydrate snack is required to prevent hypoglycemia during activity. (C) is incorrect as it is not a total contraindication, but rather a situation requiring a snack.

questionText: A patient is 6 weeks post-op from a massive rotator cuff repair (supraspinatus and infraspinatus). According to standard rehabilitation protocols, which of the following activities is MOST appropriate at this stage?

options.A: Initiation of active-assisted range of motion (AAROM) in the supine position.
options.B: Progression to active range of motion (AROM) in gravity-minimized planes.
options.C: Resisted strengthening using light dumbbells (1-2 lbs).
options.D: Heavy eccentric loading to promote tendon-to-bone healing.

explanation: By 6 weeks, most massive repair protocols transition from the protection/passive phase to the active-assisted or early active phase. (B) is correct; this is the 'Intermediate Phase' where AROM is introduced. (C) and (D) are premature and could jeopardize the surgical repair site.

questionText: A patient with Relapsing-Remitting Multiple Sclerosis (RRMS) presents with an 'internuclear ophthalmoplegia' (INO). When the patient attempts to look to the left, the right eye fails to adduct, and there is nystagmus in the left eye. Where is the lesion MOST likely located?

options.B: Left Oculomotor Nerve (CN III).
options.C: Right Medial Longitudinal Fasciculus (MLF).
options.D: Left Medial Longitudinal Fasciculus (MLF).

explanation: (C) is correct. INO is caused by a lesion in the MLF, which coordinates horizontal eye movements. In INO, the eye *ipsilateral* to the MLF lesion cannot adduct during conjugate lateral gaze. If the right eye cannot adduct when looking left, the right MLF is the site of demyelination.

questionText: During a physical therapy session, a patient with a history of peripheral artery disease (PAD) describes a new onset of sudden, severe pain in the left foot. The foot appears pale and is cold to the touch. The dorsalis pedis pulse is absent. What is the MOST likely diagnosis and necessary action?

options.A: Venous Thromboembolism; elevate the leg and apply heat.
options.B: Chronic Venous Insufficiency; apply 30-40 mmHg compression stockings.
options.C: Stable Intermittent Claudication; encourage the patient to walk through the pain.
options.D: Acute Arterial Occlusion; this is a surgical emergency requiring immediate referral.

explanation: The '6 Ps' of acute arterial occlusion are Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, and Poikilothermia (cold). (D) is correct because this indicates a sudden blockage of blood flow that threatens limb viability. (C) is incorrect as claudication pain is predictable and relieved by rest.

questionText: A 30-year-old construction worker reports lateral elbow pain when gripping tools. The therapist performs a test where the patient is asked to resist extension of the third digit (middle finger). This maneuvers reproduces the patient's lateral elbow pain. Which muscle is MOST specifically implicated by this test?

options.B: Extensor Carpi Radialis Brevis
options.C: Extensor Digitorum Communis
options.D: Supinator

explanation: Maudsley's test (resisting 3rd digit extension) specifically stresses the Extensor Carpi Radialis Brevis (ECRB) because it is the primary muscle involved in lateral epicondylalgia and has a secondary attachment that is loaded during this maneuver. (B) is correct.

questionText: A patient with a spinal cord injury at the C6 level (ASIA A) is learning to perform a pressure relief maneuver. Which of the following muscles is primarily utilized to perform a 'sitting push-up' or weight shift in this patient?

options.B: Triceps Brachii and Latissimus Dorsi.
options.C: Serratus Anterior and Lower Trapezius.
options.D: Flexor Carpi Ulnaris and Triceps.

explanation: C6 patients lack triceps (C7). (A) is correct because they must use 'closed-chain' mechanics, utilizing the anterior deltoid and pectoralis major to stabilize the shoulder and lock the elbows into extension via external rotation to lift the pelvis. (B) would be for a C7-C8 level patient.

questionText: A patient is referred to physical therapy for wound management on the medial malleolus. The wound is shallow with irregular borders, significant yellow slough, and the surrounding skin is brownish (hemosiderin staining). What is the MOST appropriate primary intervention?

options.A: Sharp debridement followed by an airtight occlusive dressing.
options.B: Compression therapy (e.g., short-stretch bandages) after ensuring an adequate ABI.
options.C: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy to improve local tissue perfusion.
options.D: Daily Whirlpool treatments to remove slough and increase circulation.

explanation: The description (medial malleolus, irregular borders, hemosiderin staining) is classic for a venous insufficiency ulcer. (B) is correct because compression is the 'gold standard' for venous ulcers to reduce edema and improve valve function. (D) is outdated and generally contraindicated for chronic wound management.

questionText: A 16-year-old female dancer presents with snapping in the lateral hip during active hip flexion and extension. The therapist suspects 'External Snapping Hip' (Coxa Saltans). Which structure is MOST likely snapping over the greater trochanter?

options.B: Rectus femoris tendon.
options.C: Iliotibial band or Gluteus maximus tendon.
options.D: Ligamentum teres.

explanation: External snapping hip (C) involves the ITB or the gluteus maximus tendon snapping over the greater trochanter. (A) describes 'Internal' snapping hip, which occurs as the iliopsoas tendon snaps over the pectineal eminence or femoral head.

questionText: A 70-year-old male is 2 days post-op from an abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) repair. During a bedside evaluation, he becomes tachypneic and complains of sudden pleuritic chest pain. His heart rate is 115 bpm and his oxygen saturation has dropped to 89%. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.B: Pulmonary Embolism.
options.C: Pneumothorax.
options.D: Post-operative Atelectasis.

explanation: (B) is correct. The sudden onset of tachypnea, pleuritic chest pain, and hypoxia in a post-surgical patient is the classic triad for a pulmonary embolism (PE). Tachycardia is the most common ECG/vitals finding. (D) would present more gradually and with less acute pain.

questionText: During an evaluation of a patient with suspected L5 nerve root compression, the therapist performs the Reflex test. Which of the following reflexes would MOST likely be diminished if L5 is involved?

options.B: Achilles (S1).
options.C: Hamstrings (S1-S2).
options.D: None; L5 does not have a reliable, standard deep tendon reflex (DTR).

explanation: (D) is correct. While L4 (Patellar) and S1 (Achilles) have clear, standard DTRs, the L5 nerve root does not have a reliable reflex associated with it in common clinical practice (though some use the Tibialis Posterior, it is not standard). Diagnosis of L5 involvement relies on myotomes (EHL) and dermatomes.

questionText: A patient with Grave's Disease is referred to PT for general conditioning. Which of the following exercise considerations is MOST important for this patient based on their primary pathology?

options.A: Monitor for bradycardia and cold intolerance.
options.B: Avoid weight-bearing exercise due to high risk of stress fractures from osteomalacia.
options.C: Monitor for heat intolerance, tachycardia, and increased risk of arrhythmias.
options.D: Encourage high-intensity interval training to manage weight gain.

explanation: Grave's Disease is a form of Hyperthyroidism. (C) is correct; these patients have an increased metabolic rate, leading to tachycardia, heat intolerance, and potential cardiac stress. (A) and (D) are more appropriate for Hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's).

questionText: A physical therapist is performing the 'Slump Test' on a patient with suspected lumbar disc herniation. The patient reports reproduction of their leg pain when the neck is flexed. What is the final step the therapist should take to confirm that the pain is of neural/dural origin?

options.A: Increase the pressure on the patient's shoulders.
options.B: Ask the patient to extend their neck and see if the leg pain decreases.
options.C: Perform a forceful ankle plantarflexion.
options.D: Ask the patient to cough to increase intrathecal pressure.

explanation: (B) is correct. Structural differentiation is key to the Slump Test. By moving a joint far away from the site of pain (the neck) and observing a change in distal symptoms (the leg), the therapist confirms that the tension is being transmitted through the continuous neural tissues.

questionText: A patient with a complete T6 Spinal Cord Injury is working on advanced wheelchair skills. According to the expected functional outcomes for this level, which of the following is the MOST difficult skill this patient should be able to perform independently?

options.B: Transfer from wheelchair to car.
options.C: Independent manual wheelchair propulsion over level surfaces.
options.D: Transfer from the floor into their wheelchair.

explanation: While T6 patients have full UE control and some trunk control, (D) floor-to-wheelchair transfers are highly demanding and considered an advanced skill for this level. (A, B, C) are typically achieved earlier in rehab.

questionText: A patient is being treated for a venous stasis ulcer. The therapist notes that the patient's legs are very edematous and the skin is tight and shiny. Which of the following is a direct contraindication for applying intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC)?

options.B: Chronic venous insufficiency.
options.C: Controlled Hypertension.
options.D: History of a healed DVT from 5 years ago.

explanation: (A) is correct. IPC increases venous return and pushes fluid back into central circulation. If a patient has acute pulmonary edema or CHF, the heart cannot handle the increased preload, which can lead to worsening respiratory distress or heart failure.

questionText: A 22-year-old male with ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is beginning a PT program. Which of the following is the MOST important long-term focus of his exercise routine?

options.A: High-impact aerobic training to maintain bone density.
options.B: Extension-based exercises and postural training to counteract the progression of kyphosis.
options.C: Heavy resistance training for the rectus abdominis.
options.D: Prolonged sitting with a focus on lumbar flexion stretching.

explanation: AS causes progressive fusion and kyphosis of the spine. (B) is correct; extension and postural awareness are critical to maintaining as much upright function as possible. Flexion-based exercises (D) would accelerate the deformity.

questionText: A patient presents with a 'foot drop' after a total knee arthroplasty. Examination shows weakness in ankle dorsiflexion and eversion, and sensory loss between the first and second toes. Which nerve was MOST likely compressed during the surgery?

options.B: Superficial Peroneal Nerve.
options.C: Common Peroneal (Fibular) Nerve.
options.D: Femoral Nerve.

explanation: (C) is correct. The common peroneal nerve wraps around the fibular head and is vulnerable to compression (from tourniquets or positioning). Its branches control dorsiflexion (Deep branch) and eversion (Superficial branch). Loss of both, plus the specific web-space sensation, confirms the Common Peroneal Nerve.

questionText: A patient with a history of asthma is using a Peak Flow Meter. Their 'personal best' is 500 L/min. Today, they record a value of 350 L/min. According to the Asthma Action Plan zones, what does this indicate and what is the action?

options.B: Yellow Zone (50-80% of best); use rescue inhaler and contact physician.
options.C: Red Zone (< 50% of best); seek immediate emergency care.
options.D: Normal diurnal variation; no action needed.

explanation: 350 L/min is 70% of 500 L/min. (B) is correct; the Yellow Zone (50-80%) indicates a caution/flare-up and requires the use of quick-relief medication and monitoring.

questionText: A 40-year-old female presents with pain in the bottom of her heel that is most severe during the first few steps in the morning. Upon examination, she has limited ankle dorsiflexion (5°) with the knee extended. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate initial intervention?

options.A: Gastrocnemius stretching and calf-focused eccentric loading.
options.B: Application of a custom-molded rigid orthotic.
options.C: Immediate referral for a corticosteroid injection.
options.D: Complete non-weight bearing for 2 weeks in a CAM boot.

explanation: The symptoms (morning pain) indicate Plantar Fasciitis. (A) is correct; tight calves are a major risk factor and contributor. Stretching the gastroc/soleus and the fascia itself has strong evidence for initial management. (B) is often secondary to off-the-shelf options first.

questionText: A patient with a brain tumor exhibits 'ataxic' breathing (irregular rate and depth with periods of apnea). Which part of the brain is MOST likely affected by the lesion or increased pressure?

options.B: Midbrain.
options.C: Medulla Oblongata.
options.D: Hypothalamus.

explanation: (C) is correct. The medulla oblongata contains the primary respiratory centers (dorsal and ventral respiratory groups) that control the rhythm and depth of breathing. Lesions here cause highly irregular, ataxic breathing patterns (Biot's respiration).

questionText: A 50-year-old patient who is morbidly obese undergoes a major abdominal surgery. On post-op day 1, which of the following is the MOST effective intervention to prevent post-operative atelectasis?

options.A: Supine bed rest for 48 hours to allow the incision to heal.
options.B: Manual percussion and vibration in the Trendelenburg position.
options.C: Early mobilization (sitting on the edge of the bed and walking) and incentive spirometry.
options.D: Coughing with a 'huff' technique while in the prone position.

explanation: (C) is correct. Early mobilization is the gold standard for post-op pulmonary care to increase Functional Residual Capacity and prevent lung collapse. Incentive spirometry provides visual feedback for deep breathing.

questionText: A patient with Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is participating in a PT session. Which of the following is a critical vital sign consideration for this patient during exercise?

options.A: They will likely demonstrate an exaggerated drop in blood pressure with exertion.
options.B: BP should be monitored closely, but never measured on the arm with an arteriovenous (AV) fistula.
options.C: Their heart rate will likely be significantly lower than age-predicted maximums due to uremia.
options.D: Weight should be measured monthly to monitor for muscle wasting.

explanation: (B) is correct. Standard of care for dialysis/CKD patients is to avoid using the limb with the AV fistula for BP or blood draws to prevent damage/clotting of the access site. Weight is usually measured *daily* to monitor for fluid retention.

questionText: During a physical examination of the knee, the therapist notes that the patient has 'Genu Varum.' This alignment is MOST likely to lead to increased compressive forces in which compartment of the knee?

options.B: Lateral compartment.
options.C: Patellofemoral compartment.
options.D: Posterior compartment.

explanation: Genu varum (bow-leggedness) shifts the mechanical load line medially. (A) is correct; this increases the stress on the medial meniscus and medial articular cartilage, often leading to medial compartment osteoarthritis.

questionText: A patient with Parkinson's Disease is experiencing 'off' periods where they become very stiff and have difficulty moving. Which of the following neurotransmitter imbalances is the primary cause of this condition?

options.A: Decreased dopamine in the substantia nigra pars compacta.
options.B: Increased acetylcholine in the motor cortex.
options.C: Decreased GABA in the thalamus.
options.D: Increased serotonin in the raphe nuclei.

explanation: (A) is correct. PD is defined by the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, which leads to over-activity of the 'indirect pathway' and under-activity of the 'direct pathway' in the basal ganglia, resulting in bradykinesia and rigidity.

questionText: A 55-year-old male with a history of hypertension and smoking presents with sudden, sharp, 'tearing' pain between his shoulder blades. His blood pressure is 190/110 mmHg. What is the MOST likely medical emergency?

options.B: Acute Myocardial Infarction.
options.C: Aortic Dissection.
options.D: Esophageal Rupture.

explanation: (C) is correct. The description of 'tearing' or 'ripping' pain radiating to the back in a hypertensive patient is highly classic for an aortic dissection, a life-threatening emergency. (B) usually presents as pressure or crushing pain in the chest/arm.

questionText: A 28-year-old female in her third trimester of pregnancy reports sharp pain in the pubic region when walking and climbing stairs. What is the MOST likely cause and appropriate intervention?

options.A: Round ligament pain; encourage high-impact exercise to strengthen the core.
options.B: Symphysis Pubis Dysfunction (SPD); use a pelvic support belt and avoid wide-legged movements.
options.C: Sciatica; perform aggressive hamstring stretching.
options.D: Diastasis Recti; perform 100 crunches daily.

explanation: (B) is correct. SPD is caused by the hormonal loosening of the pelvic ligaments (relaxin). Management focuses on stabilization (belts) and avoiding aggravating activities like unilateral weight bearing or wide abduction. (D) would be contraindicated for Diastasis Recti.

questionText: A patient with limited wrist extension and pain over the dorsal-radial aspect of the wrist is diagnosed with 'Wrist Impingement.' During mobilization, which direction of glide of the proximal row of carpals on the radius would BEST improve wrist extension?

options.B: Dorsal glide.
options.C: Radial glide.
options.D: Ulnar glide.

explanation: According to the convex-concave rule, the convex carpals move on the concave radius. Extension involves a dorsal roll and a volar glide. (A) is correct to improve extension. (B) would improve flexion.

questionText: A patient who had a traumatic brain injury 6 months ago demonstrates a 'shuffling' gait and has difficulty initiating movement. Which of the following 'Secondary Parkinsonism' causes is MOST likely following a head injury?

options.B: Multiple System Atrophy.
options.C: Post-traumatic parkinsonism due to diffuse axonal injury in the basal ganglia.
options.D: Vascular parkinsonism.

explanation: (C) is correct. Severe head trauma can damage the dopaminergic pathways or the basal ganglia directly, leading to symptoms that mimic Parkinson's Disease. This is a recognized secondary cause of the syndrome.

questionText: A 67-year-old patient presents with progressive bilateral leg weakness over 3 months, absent ankle reflexes, intact sensation, and elevated cerebrospinal fluid protein (120 mg/dL) with normal cell count. EMG shows demyelination. The patient recently recovered from Campylobacter jejuni infection. Which finding would be MOST unexpected in this clinical presentation?

options.A: Ascending pattern of weakness starting distally
options.B: Respiratory muscle involvement requiring ventilatory support
options.C: Hyperreflexia and positive Babinski sign bilaterally
options.D: Autonomic dysfunction with blood pressure fluctuations

explanation: Option C is correct as the UNEXPECTED finding. This presentation is classic for Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS): post-infectious (Campylobacter), ascending weakness, areflexia, albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated CSF protein, normal cells), and EMG demyelination. GBS is a LOWER motor neuron disorder causing HYPOreflexia or areflexia, NOT hyperreflexia. Positive Babinski (upper motor neuron sign) would be completely inconsistent with GBS. Options A, B, and D are all EXPECTED findings in GBS: ascending weakness is typical, respiratory failure occurs in 20-30% requiring ventilation, and autonomic dysfunction is common.

questionText: A 58-year-old patient with severe COPD (FEV1 32% predicted) demonstrates the following arterial blood gas values at rest on room air: pH 7.38, PaCO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 33 mEq/L, PaO2 62 mmHg. What do these values indicate?

options.A: Acute respiratory acidosis requiring immediate intervention
options.B: Metabolic alkalosis from diuretic use
options.C: Normal blood gas values
options.D: Compensated respiratory acidosis (chronic CO2 retention)

explanation: Option D is correct. This patient shows compensated respiratory acidosis: pH is near normal (7.38, slightly low but within 7.35-7.45 range), PaCO2 is elevated (normal 35-45 mmHg), and HCO3 is elevated (normal 22-26 mEq/L). The elevated bicarbonate represents metabolic compensation for chronic CO2 retention - kidneys retain HCO3 to buffer the respiratory acidosis, normalizing pH. This is typical in severe COPD with chronic hypercapnia. Option A is incorrect because pH is compensated, not acutely acidotic (<7.35). Option B is wrong; this is respiratory, not metabolic. Option C is incorrect due to abnormal PaCO2 and HCO3.

questionText: A 34-year-old patient presents with chronic low back pain. Examination reveals positive straight leg raise at 35 degrees on the right reproducing leg pain, absent right Achilles reflex, weakness of right ankle plantarflexion (3/5), and numbness on the plantar surface of the right foot. Which nerve root is MOST likely affected?

options.B: L4
options.C: L5
options.D: S1

explanation: Option D is correct. This is classic S1 radiculopathy: weak plantarflexion (gastrocnemius/soleus are S1-S2), absent Achilles reflex (S1), plantar foot numbness (S1 dermatome), positive SLR. The confusing option is C (L5), which causes foot drop and dorsiflexion weakness, but L5 has DORSAL foot numbness, normal Achilles reflex, and weak dorsiflexion/eversion, not plantarflexion. Option A (L3) affects anterior thigh and weak knee extension. Option B (L4) affects medial leg, weak knee extension, diminished patellar reflex. The plantarflexion weakness and absent Achilles reflex are key discriminators for S1.

questionText: A 72-year-old patient has a wound on the lateral malleolus with the following characteristics: well-defined borders, minimal drainage, pale wound bed, very painful, hairless skin surrounding wound, and diminished pedal pulses. ABI is 0.58. Which treatment is CONTRAINDICATED?

options.B: Compression therapy at 30-40 mmHg
options.C: Referral to vascular surgery for revascularization
options.D: Gentle range of motion exercises

explanation: Option B is correct as CONTRAINDICATED. This is clearly an arterial ulcer (lateral location, well-defined borders, pale base, very painful, diminished pulses) with ABI 0.58 indicating MODERATE peripheral arterial disease. Compression therapy is absolutely contraindicated with ABI <0.80 as it will further compromise already inadequate arterial blood flow, potentially causing tissue necrosis or limb loss. The confusing options are A and D which seem conservative but are actually appropriate for arterial ulcers. Option C is the MOST appropriate treatment - revascularization is needed for healing. Option A protects wound without compression.

questionText: A 45-year-old patient underwent transtibial amputation 6 weeks ago. The patient reports burning pain in the foot that was amputated, rated 7/10, worse at night. The residual limb is well-healed with no signs of infection. What is this phenomenon called?

options.B: Neuroma pain
options.C: Phantom limb pain
options.D: Psychosomatic pain

explanation: Option C is correct. Phantom limb pain is pain perceived in the part of the limb that has been amputated, occurring in 60-80% of amputees. It's real neuropathic pain (not psychosomatic) caused by neuroplastic changes in brain and spinal cord. Described as burning, cramping, or shooting. Distinguished from residual limb pain (Option A, which is pain in the remaining stump from tissue, bone, or wound issues). Option B (neuroma) causes localized stump pain with palpable tender nodule. Option D is incorrect and dismissive - phantom pain is a real neurological phenomenon requiring treatment (medications, mirror therapy, desensitization).

questionText: A 63-year-old patient in cardiac rehabilitation demonstrates the following during exercise: HR increases from 72 bpm to 82 bpm over 10 minutes of treadmill walking at moderate intensity (4 METs). What does this heart rate response suggest?

options.A: Normal heart rate response to exercise
options.B: Excessive tachycardia requiring exercise termination
options.C: Patient needs higher exercise intensity
options.D: Chronotropic incompetence (blunted heart rate response)
